description.text: Albumin is the most abundant plasma protein (55-65% of total). Synthesized by the liver.
Maintains oncotic pressure, preventing fluid from leaking into tissues.
Transports hormones, drugs, calcium, fatty acids, and bilirubin.
Marker of: Liver function, nutritional status, kidney disease (nephrotic syndrome).

high.text: High albumin: Usually dehydration (hemoconcentration). Rarely clinically significant.

low.text: Low albumin: Liver disease (cirrhosis, hepatitis), kidney disease (nephrotic syndrome), malnutrition, chronic inflammation, burns, protein-losing enteropathy.
<2.5 g/dL: Severe hypoalbuminemia. Edema/ascites likely. Increased drug sensitivity.

remarks.text: 3.5-5.0 g/dL: Normal (Mayo Clinic). Reference range for ≥12 months.
3.0-3.5 g/dL: Mild decrease. Early malnutrition or mild liver/kidney disease.
2.5-3.0 g/dL: Moderate decrease. Significant liver disease, nephrotic syndrome, or malnutrition.
<2.5 g/dL: Severe hypoalbuminemia. Edema likely. Drug sensitivity increased.
<2.0 g/dL: Critical. High risk of complications. May require albumin infusion.
35-50 g/L = 3.5-5.0 g/dL (SI units)
<25 g/L = <2.5 g/dL (severe hypoalbuminemia)

description.text: ALP (Alkaline Phosphatase) is found in liver, bile ducts, bone, kidney, and intestine. Elevated in cholestasis and bone disease.
Alkalische Phosphatase kommt in Leber, Knochen, Niere und Darm vor.
If ALP elevated, check GGT: If GGT also high = liver/bile duct source. If GGT normal = bone source.

high.text: Elevated ALP: Cholestasis (bile duct obstruction), primary biliary cholangitis, bone disease (Paget, fractures, tumors), pregnancy, growing children.
Very high (>4× ULN): Suggests biliary obstruction or primary sclerosing cholangitis.

low.text: Low ALP: Rare. Hypophosphatasia, malnutrition, zinc deficiency, hypothyroidism.

remarks.text: Adult ALP 40-130 U/L per DGKL/VDGH 2005 consensus
Mild elevation 130-390 U/L (1-3× ULN). May be physiological or pathological.
Moderate elevation 390-1300 U/L (3-10× ULN). Suggests cholestasis or bone disease.
Severe elevation >1300 U/L (>10× ULN). Suggests biliary obstruction or severe bone disease.
Pregnancy 3rd trimester: Up to 250 U/L is physiological (placental ALP)

description.text: Alpha-1 globulin fraction contains primarily alpha-1-antitrypsin (AAT), an acute phase reactant.
Low levels: Alpha-1-antitrypsin deficiency (genetic), severe liver disease.
High levels: Acute inflammation, pregnancy, steroid therapy.

high.text: High alpha-1: Acute inflammation, pregnancy, steroid therapy.

low.text: Low alpha-1: Alpha-1-antitrypsin deficiency (genetic), severe liver disease, protein-losing states.

remarks.text: 2.9-4.9% of total protein
0.1-0.3 g/dL (Medscape). Contains alpha-1-antitrypsin (AAT).
<0.1 g/dL: Alpha-1-antitrypsin deficiency. Genetic condition causing lung/liver disease.
1-3 g/L = 0.1-0.3 g/dL (SI units)

description.text: Alpha-2 globulin contains alpha-2-macroglobulin and haptoglobin (binds free hemoglobin).
Haptoglobin is an acute phase reactant that binds hemoglobin released from hemolysis.
Increased in: Nephrotic syndrome (large proteins retained), inflammation, steroids.
Decreased haptoglobin: Hemolysis (intravascular), liver disease.

high.text: High alpha-2: Nephrotic syndrome (hallmark), acute inflammation, steroid therapy.

low.text: Low alpha-2: Hemolysis (low haptoglobin), severe liver disease, malnutrition.

remarks.text: 7.1-11.8% of total protein
0.6-1.0 g/dL (Medscape). Contains alpha-2-macroglobulin and haptoglobin.
>1.0 g/dL: Elevated. Nephrotic syndrome (large proteins retained), acute inflammation.

description.text: Amylase digests carbohydrates. Elevated in pancreatitis but also in salivary gland disease and bowel obstruction.
Amylasen spalten Kohlenhydrate und sind bei Pankreatitis erhöht.
>3× ULN suggests pancreatitis, but lipase is more specific. Also elevated in mumps, intestinal obstruction, macroamylasemia.

high.text: Elevated amylase: Acute pancreatitis (most common), chronic pancreatitis, salivary gland disease (mumps), bowel obstruction, perforated ulcer, macroamylasemia.

low.text: Low amylase: Rarely significant. Pancreatic insufficiency, severe liver disease.

remarks.text: Amylase 28-100 U/L is normal per DGKL. Varies by laboratory method.
100-300 U/L: Mild elevation. May be non-specific or early pancreatitis.
>300 U/L (>3× ULN): Suggests pancreatitis. Check lipase for confirmation.
>1000 U/L: Severe pancreatitis or other causes (salivary, intestinal).

description.text: ALT (Alanine Aminotransferase) is the most liver-specific enzyme. Best marker for hepatocellular injury.
ALT (GPT) ist das leberspezifischste Enzym und der beste Marker für Leberzellschäden.
ALT > AST suggests viral hepatitis or NAFLD. ALT <30 U/L is optimal for metabolic health.

high.text: Elevated ALT: Fatty liver (NAFLD - most common), viral hepatitis, alcohol, medications (statins, antibiotics), muscle injury.
ALT > AST: Typical for viral hepatitis, NAFLD. AST > ALT: Typical for alcoholic liver disease.

low.text: Low ALT: Rarely significant. May indicate B6 deficiency, chronic kidney disease, or normal variant.

remarks.text: Male ALT 10-50 U/L per DGKL/VDGH 2005. Note: Optimal functional range <30 U/L.
Female ALT 7-35 U/L per DGKL/VDGH 2005
Optimal functional range <30 U/L (functional medicine). >30 suggests fatty liver or metabolic syndrome.
Mild elevation 1-3× ULN. Fatty liver (most common), medications, mild hepatitis.
Moderate elevation 3-10× ULN. Acute hepatitis, drug toxicity, worsening fatty liver.
>10× ULN (>500). Acute viral hepatitis, drug-induced liver injury, ischemic hepatitis.
>1000 U/L: Critical, life-threatening. Acute liver failure, severe drug toxicity.

description.text: ASO measures antibodies against streptolysin O, a toxin produced by Group A Streptococcus (GAS) bacteria.
Used to diagnose post-streptococcal complications: rheumatic fever, glomerulonephritis.
Antibodies rise 1-3 weeks after infection, peak at 3-5 weeks, decline over months.

high.text: High ASO: Recent streptococcal infection. Associated with rheumatic fever, post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis.
False positives: Liver disease, tuberculosis,某些 bacterial endocarditis.

low.text: Low/negative ASO: No recent streptococcal infection OR infection was >6 months ago.
Early infection: ASO may be negative in first week of infection. Repeat test in 1-2 weeks.

remarks.text: Adult: <200 IU/mL (<166 Todd units). Negative test indicates no recent streptococcal infection.
<5 years: ≤70 IU/mL per Mayo Clinic
5-17 years: ≤640 IU/mL per Mayo Clinic
≥18 years: ≤530 IU/mL per Mayo Clinic
200-400 IU/mL: Elevated. Suggests recent streptococcal infection within past 1-5 weeks.
>400 IU/mL: Significantly elevated. Strong evidence of recent streptococcal infection.

description.text: AST (Aspartate Aminotransferase) found in liver, heart, muscle, kidney, brain. Less specific for liver than ALT.
AST (GOT) kommt in Leber, Herzmuskel, Skelettmuskulatur vor. Weniger leberspezifisch als ALT.
AST/ALT ratio >2 suggests alcohol-related liver disease. AST also elevated in heart attack and muscle injury.

high.text: Elevated AST: Liver disease (hepatitis, cirrhosis), alcohol use, muscle injury (exercise, trauma), heart attack, medications.
AST > ALT: Suggests alcohol-related liver disease or cirrhosis (De Ritis ratio >2).

low.text: Low AST: Rarely significant. May indicate B6 deficiency or chronic kidney disease.

remarks.text: Male AST 15-40 U/L per DGKL/VDGH 2005
Female AST 13-35 U/L per DGKL/VDGH 2005
Mild elevation 1-3× ULN. Fatty liver, medications, alcohol, muscle injury.
Moderate elevation 3-10× ULN. Hepatitis, drug toxicity, significant muscle injury.
>10× ULN. Acute viral hepatitis, ischemic hepatitis, drug toxicity, severe muscle injury.
AST > ALT (ratio >2) suggests alcohol-related liver disease (De Ritis ratio)

description.text: Basophils are the least common white blood cells. Involved in allergic reactions and inflammation.
Basophile sind die seltensten weißen Blutkörperchen und enthalten Histamin.
High basophils: Rare. May indicate chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) or hypothyroidism.

high.text: Basophilia: Myeloproliferative neoplasms (especially CML), hypothyroidism, allergic reactions, chronic inflammation.

low.text: Basopenia: Rarely significant. May occur with acute infections, steroids, hyperthyroidism.

remarks.text: Basophils 0-2% of total WBC per NHS guidelines
>2%: Basophilia. Rare. Myeloproliferative disorders, hypothyroidism.
Absolute basophil count 0.0-0.1 ×10^9/L
>0.1: Basophilia. Myeloproliferative neoplasms (CML), hypothyroidism.

description.text: Beta globulin combines beta-1 and beta-2 fractions. Contains transferrin and complement proteins.
Elevated in: Iron deficiency, multiple myeloma, nephrotic syndrome.
Decreased in: Malnutrition, chronic liver disease.

high.text: High beta globulin: Iron deficiency, multiple myeloma, nephrotic syndrome, complement activation.

low.text: Low beta globulin: Malnutrition, chronic liver disease, protein-losing states.

remarks.text: 7.9-13.9% of total protein
0.7-1.1 g/dL (combined beta-1 + beta-2).
>1.1 g/dL: Elevated. Iron deficiency, multiple myeloma, nephrotic syndrome.

description.text: Beta-1 globulin contains primarily transferrin (iron transport protein).
Elevated in: Iron deficiency (transferrin increased), pregnancy, oral contraceptives.
Decreased in: Chronic inflammation, liver disease, malnutrition.

high.text: High beta-1: Iron deficiency (transferrin increased), pregnancy, oral contraceptives.

low.text: Low beta-1: Chronic inflammation, liver disease, malnutrition.

remarks.text: 4.7-7.2% of total protein
0.4-0.6 g/dL. Contains primarily transferrin (iron transport).
>0.6 g/dL: Elevated beta-1 suggests iron deficiency (transferrin increased).

description.text: Beta-2 globulin contains beta-2-microglobulin (light chain component) and complement C3.
Elevated in: Multiple myeloma, Waldenström macroglobulinemia, chronic kidney disease.
Beta-2-microglobulin is a marker of kidney function and lymphoid malignancies.

high.text: High beta-2: Multiple myeloma, Waldenström macroglobulinemia, chronic kidney disease, lymphomas.

low.text: Low beta-2: Rare. May indicate immunodeficiency or complement deficiency.

remarks.text: 3.2-6.5% of total protein
0.2-0.5 g/dL. Contains beta-2-microglobulin, complement C3.
>0.5 g/dL: Elevated. Multiple myeloma, Waldenström macroglobulinemia, kidney disease.

description.text: Blood Sedimentation Rate (BSG/ESR) measures how quickly red blood cells settle in a test tube. Non-specific marker of inflammation.
Die Blutsenkungsgeschwindigkeit (BSG) ist ein unspezifischer Entzündungsparameter.
BSG rises slowly (24-48h) and returns to normal slowly. Less specific than CRP but useful for monitoring chronic conditions.

high.text: Elevated BSG: Inflammation, infection, autoimmune disease, malignancy, anemia, pregnancy, elderly age.
Very high (>100): Consider temporal arteritis, multiple myeloma, severe infection, advanced malignancy.

low.text: Low BSG: Polycythemia, sickle cell anemia, hypofibrinogenemia, spherocytosis. Rarely clinically significant.

remarks.text: Male BSG <15 mm/1st hour is normal per NHS guidelines
Female BSG <20 mm/1st hour is normal per NHS guidelines
Elderly males: Upper limit = age/2 (e.g., 70yo = 35 mm/h)
Elderly females: Upper limit = (age+10)/2 (e.g., 70yo = 40 mm/h)
20-50 mm/h: Moderate elevation (mild inflammation, anemia, pregnancy)
50-100 mm/h: Marked elevation (active inflammation, infection, autoimmune disease)
>100 mm/h: Severe elevation (severe infection, malignancy, temporal arteritis)

description.text: CA 19-9 is the most extensively validated pancreatic cancer biomarker. Also called sialyl-Lewis A.
NOT a screening test for asymptomatic patients. Used for diagnosis in symptomatic patients, monitoring treatment, and detecting recurrence.
Cannot be used in Lewis antigen-negative individuals (5-10% of population) who cannot produce CA 19-9.

high.text: High CA 19-9: Pancreatic cancer (most common), cholangiocarcinoma, gastric cancer. Benign: pancreatitis, biliary obstruction, cirrhosis.
Pre-operative levels >37 U/mL associated with shorter survival; normal levels associated with median survival 32-36 months.

low.text: Low CA 19-9: Normal finding or Lewis antigen-negative status (cannot produce CA 19-9). Does not rule out cancer.
10-15% of pancreatic cancer patients have normal CA 19-9 levels at diagnosis.

remarks.text: <37 U/mL: Normal. At cutoff 37 U/mL: Sensitivity 81%, Specificity 90% for pancreatic cancer.
37-100 U/mL: Mild elevation. May be benign biliary disease, pancreatitis, or early malignancy.
100-1000 U/mL: Moderate elevation. Suspicious for malignancy, particularly pancreatic cancer.
>1000 U/mL: Highly elevated. Strongly suggests pancreatic or biliary malignancy.
>1000 U/mL: At this level, sensitivity drops to 41% but specificity approaches 100%.

description.text: Calcium is essential for bone health, muscle contraction, nerve transmission, and blood clotting. 99% stored in bones.
Calcium ist wichtig für Knochen, Muskeln und Nervenfunktion.
Total calcium depends on albumin. Correct for albumin: Adjusted Ca = Measured Ca + 0.02 × (40 - Albumin in g/L). Or check ionized calcium.

high.text: Hypercalcemia: Primary hyperparathyroidism, malignancy (bone mets), vitamin D excess, thiazide diuretics, sarcoidosis.

low.text: Hypocalcemia: Hypoparathyroidism, vitamin D deficiency, chronic kidney disease, hypoalbuminemia, pancreatitis.

remarks.text: Total calcium 2.20-2.60 mmol/L (8.8-10.4 mg/dL). Ionized Ca more physiologically relevant.
Optimal range 2.35-2.50 mmol/L for bone health and neuromuscular function.
Mild hypocalcemia 2.10-2.20. May be asymptomatic. Check albumin and ionized Ca.
Hypocalcemia 1.90-2.10. Paresthesias, muscle cramps. Requires treatment.
Severe hypocalcemia <1.90. Tetany, seizures, QT prolongation. EMERGENCY.
Hypercalcemia 2.60-3.00. Fatigue, constipation, polyuria. Check PTH.
Severe hypercalcemia >3.00. Confusion, arrhythmias, coma. EMERGENCY.

description.text: CEA is an oncofetal glycoprotein. Normally produced during fetal development, levels fall after birth.
NOT a screening test for cancer. Used primarily for monitoring treatment response and detecting recurrence in known cancer patients.
Elevated in 70% of colorectal cancers, but also in pancreatic, gastric, lung, and breast cancers. Benign causes: smoking, liver disease, IBD.

high.text: High CEA: Colorectal cancer (most common), pancreatic, gastric, lung, breast cancer. Also benign: smoking, cirrhosis, hepatitis, pancreatitis, IBD.
Trend is more important than single value. Rising CEA during treatment suggests progression; falling suggests response.

low.text: Low CEA: Normal finding. Cannot rule out cancer - only 70% of colorectal cancers have elevated CEA.

remarks.text: Non-smoker: <3.9 ng/mL per Labcorp. Some labs use <2.5 ng/mL.
Smoker: <5.6 ng/mL. Smoking increases CEA levels. Advise smoking cessation before testing.
3.9-10 ng/mL: Mild elevation. May be benign (smoking, liver disease) or early malignancy.
10-20 ng/mL: Moderate elevation. Suggests malignancy or significant benign disease.
>20 ng/mL: Significant elevation. Strongly suggests malignancy, especially colorectal cancer.
>5 ng/mL in cancer patients: May indicate recurrence or progression. Trend more important than single value.

description.text: Ceruloplasmin is a copper-containing protein made in the liver. It carries 90% of serum copper.
ESSENTIAL test with serum copper for Wilson disease diagnosis.
Low levels: Wilson disease, Menkes disease, severe liver disease, protein deficiency states.
High levels: Pregnancy, estrogen therapy, inflammation, cancer (acute phase reactant).

high.text: High ceruloplasmin: Pregnancy, estrogen therapy, oral contraceptives, inflammation, lymphoma, breast cancer.

low.text: Low ceruloplasmin: Wilson disease (90% of patients), Menkes disease, severe liver disease, malnutrition, protein-losing states.

remarks.text: 0.2-0.5 g/L = 20-50 mg/dL (SI units)
<0.2 g/L: Wilson disease threshold
20-40 mg/dL: Normal adult range (Medscape). 90% of serum copper is bound to ceruloplasmin.
19-31 mg/dL: Mayo Clinic adult reference range.
<20 mg/dL: Low. 90% of Wilson disease patients have levels <20 mg/dL.
<5 mg/dL: Severe deficiency. Strong indicator of Wilson disease.

description.text: Chloride is the primary extracellular anion. Maintains electrical neutrality and acid-base balance.
Chlorid ist das wichtigste Extracellular-Anion.
Usually follows sodium. Low with vomiting (HCl loss). High with dehydration or renal tubular acidosis.

high.text: Hyperchloremia: Dehydration, renal tubular acidosis, excessive saline administration, metabolic acidosis.

low.text: Hypochloremia: Vomiting (loss of HCl), diuretics, SIADH, metabolic alkalosis, heart failure.

remarks.text: Chloride 98-106 mmol/L is normal. Major extracellular anion.
Hypochloremia <98. Vomiting, diuretics, SIADH, metabolic alkalosis.
Hyperchloremia >106. Dehydration, renal tubular acidosis, excessive saline.

description.text: Cholinesterase (pseudocholinesterase) is a liver enzyme that breaks down acetylcholine. Marker of liver synthetic function.
Used to diagnose organophosphate pesticide poisoning (inhibits cholinesterase).
Also screens for succinylcholine sensitivity before anesthesia (prolonged apnea if deficient).
Two types: Acetylcholinesterase (RBCs, nerve) and pseudocholinesterase (liver, plasma).

high.text: High cholinesterase: Rare. May indicate obesity or nephrotic syndrome.

low.text: Low cholinesterase: Liver disease (cirrhosis, hepatitis), organophosphate poisoning, genetic deficiency, pregnancy, chronic inflammation.
<20% of baseline: Severe organophosphate poisoning requires urgent treatment (atropine, pralidoxime).

remarks.text: 8-18 U/mL = 8-18 kU/L (equivalent)
8-18 kU/L: Normal range (UCSF). Measures pseudocholinesterase (liver enzyme).
4-8 kU/L: Mild decrease. Early liver disease or mild OP exposure.
2-4 kU/L: Moderate decrease. Significant liver disease or OP exposure.
<2 kU/L: Severe decrease. Severe liver disease or acute OP poisoning.
<20% of baseline (<3.6 kU/L if normal 18): Severe organophosphate poisoning.

description.text: CK-MB is the cardiac-specific isoenzyme of creatine kinase. Elevated in myocardial infarction.
CK-MB ist die herzspezifische Isoform der Creatin-Kinase.
Rises 4-6 hours after MI, peaks at 24h. Now largely replaced by troponin. CK-MB/Total CK ratio >6% suggests cardiac source.

high.text: Elevated CK-MB: Myocardial infarction (MI), myocarditis, cardiac surgery, severe skeletal muscle injury, marathon running.

low.text: Low CK-MB: Normal finding.

remarks.text: CK-MB <25 U/L is normal. Cardiac-specific CK isoenzyme.
>25 U/L: Suggests myocardial injury. Check troponin for confirmation.
CK-MB index >6% (of total CK) suggests MI rather than skeletal muscle injury.

description.text: Copper is essential for iron metabolism, connective tissue formation, and central nervous system function.
90% of serum copper is bound to ceruloplasmin. Always check ceruloplasmin with copper levels.
Deficiency: Menkes syndrome (genetic), malnutrition, malabsorption. Causes anemia, neutropenia, neurological symptoms.
Toxicity: Wilson disease (genetic copper accumulation), Indian childhood cirrhosis.

high.text: High copper: Pregnancy, oral contraceptives, estrogen therapy, inflammation, copper overload (Wilson disease).

low.text: Low copper: Wilson disease, Menkes syndrome, malnutrition, malabsorption, nephrotic syndrome.

remarks.text: 70-140 µg/dL: Normal adult serum copper (Medscape). 90% bound to ceruloplasmin.
100-200 µg/dL: Alternative reference range used by some laboratories.
<70 µg/dL: Low. Suspect Wilson disease if ceruloplasmin also low.
<50 µg/dL: Severe deficiency. Menkes syndrome or severe malnutrition.
140-250 µg/dL: Elevated. Pregnancy, oral contraceptives, inflammation.
11-22 µmol/L = 70-140 µg/dL (conversion factor: 1 µmol/L = 63.55 µg/dL)
<11 µmol/L: Wilson disease suspected if ceruloplasmin <0.2 g/L

description.text: Cortisol is a steroid hormone produced by the adrenal glands. Regulates metabolism, immune response, and stress response.
Shows diurnal variation: highest in morning (6-8 AM), lowest at midnight.
High levels: Cushing syndrome, stress, pregnancy, estrogen therapy.
Low levels: Addison disease, adrenal insufficiency, hypopituitarism.
Morning level >14 µg/dL effectively rules out adrenal insufficiency.

high.text: High cortisol: Cushing syndrome (hypercortisolism), chronic stress, pregnancy, depression, alcoholism, obesity.

low.text: Low cortisol: Addison disease (primary adrenal insufficiency), hypopituitarism (secondary), steroid withdrawal.

remarks.text: 138-635 nmol/L = 5-23 µg/dL (morning)
83-359 nmol/L = 3-13 µg/dL (afternoon)
5-23 µg/dL: Normal morning (8 AM) cortisol. Diurnal variation - highest in morning.
3-13 µg/dL: Normal afternoon (4 PM) cortisol. Should be 1/3 to 2/3 of morning value.
>14 µg/dL: Rules out adrenal insufficiency.
<5 µg/dL: Suspicious for adrenal insufficiency. Requires ACTH stimulation test.

description.text: CK (Creatine Kinase) is found in heart, skeletal muscle, and brain. Elevated in muscle injury and MI.
CK kommt in Herzmuskel und Skelettmuskulatur vor.
Can be elevated 5-10× after exercise. Very high (>5000) suggests rhabdomyolysis. CK-MB fraction helps determine cardiac source.

high.text: Elevated CK: Myocardial infarction, muscle injury (exercise, trauma), rhabdomyolysis, myopathies, medications (statins), hypothyroidism.

low.text: Low CK: Rarely significant. May indicate low muscle mass, sedentary lifestyle, or steroid therapy.

remarks.text: Male CK 39-308 U/L per DGKL. Higher in males due to muscle mass.
Female CK 26-192 U/L per DGKL
Elevated after exercise: 300-2000 U/L common after strenuous exercise. Recheck in 48-72h.
>1000 U/L: Significant muscle injury, rhabdomyolysis, or myocardial infarction.
>5000 U/L: Rhabdomyolysis. Risk of acute kidney injury. EMERGENCY.

description.text: Creatinine is a waste product from muscle metabolism. Serum levels depend on muscle mass, diet, and kidney function.
Kreatinin ist ein Stoffwechselprodukt der Muskulatur und dient als Marker der Nierenfunktion.
Note: Creatinine is less accurate than eGFR or cystatin C due to variation in muscle mass.

high.text: Elevated creatinine: Reduced kidney function (GFR). Causes include CKD, AKI, dehydration, high muscle mass.
Acute kidney injury: Rapid rise in creatinine (>0.3 mg/dL in 48h or 1.5× baseline).

low.text: Low creatinine: Low muscle mass (elderly, malnutrition), pregnancy, liver disease. Does not indicate better kidney function.

remarks.text: Male creatinine 0.67-1.17 mg/dL (higher muscle mass)
Female creatinine 0.51-0.95 mg/dL
Elderly may have creatinine up to 1.3 mg/dL due to reduced muscle mass

description.text: CRP (C-reactive protein) is an acute-phase protein produced by the liver. Rises rapidly with inflammation.
CRP ist ein Akute-Phase-Protein der Leber und steigt bei Entzündungen schnell an.
hs-CRP (high sensitivity) is used for cardiovascular risk stratification. Standard CRP for infection/inflammation.

high.text: Elevated CRP: Inflammation, infection, tissue injury, cardiovascular risk. Causes include bacterial infection, autoimmune disease, trauma, surgery, myocardial infarction.
Cardiovascular risk: >2.9 mg/L = high risk. >10 mg/L suggests active inflammation - recheck when resolved.

low.text: Low CRP: Normal or low inflammation state. Cannot rule out inflammation completely (some conditions don't elevate CRP).

remarks.text: Optimal <0.5 mg/L for longevity and low cardiovascular risk
Low cardiovascular risk: <1.0 mg/L per AHA/CDC
Intermediate cardiovascular risk: 1.0-2.9 mg/L. Lifestyle modification recommended.
High cardiovascular risk: >2.9 mg/L. Consider statin therapy if LDL elevated.
10-100 mg/L: Moderate inflammation (infection, autoimmune disease, tissue injury).
>100 mg/L: Severe inflammation, bacterial infection, sepsis. Requires immediate evaluation.

description.text: Cystatin C is a low-molecular-weight protein produced by all nucleated cells. Freely filtered by kidneys.
Not affected by muscle mass, diet, age, or race - advantages over creatinine in certain populations.
KDIGO 2024 recommends when creatinine unreliable: extreme muscle mass, dietary extremes, elderly.
CKD-EPI 2021 combined creatinine-cystatin C equation provides most accurate GFR estimation.

high.text: Elevated cystatin C: Reduced GFR. Use CKD-EPI cystatin C equation to calculate eGFR.
Confirm CKD staging: Combine with creatinine for CKD-EPI 2021 combined equation (eGFRcr-cys).

low.text: Low cystatin C: Rarely significant. May indicate hyperthyroidism or corticosteroid use.

remarks.text: 18-49 years: 0.63-1.03 mg/L per Mayo Clinic. Less affected by muscle mass than creatinine.
≥50 years: 0.67-1.21 mg/L. Slightly higher with age.
1.03-1.5 mg/L: Mild elevation. Reduced GFR likely. Calculate eGFRcys.
1.5-2.0 mg/L: Moderate elevation. Significant kidney dysfunction.
>2.0 mg/L: Severe elevation. Advanced CKD. Calculate eGFRcys for staging.

description.text: D-Dimer is a fibrinolysis product (cross-linked fibrin degradation). Marker of active thrombosis.
Used primarily to RULE OUT DVT/PE in low-risk patients (>95% negative predictive value).
Age-adjusted cutoff (age × 10 ng/mL) for patients >50 years improves specificity.
Low specificity - elevated in infection, inflammation, surgery, pregnancy, cancer, elderly.

high.text: High D-Dimer: DVT, PE, DIC, recent surgery, infection, inflammation, trauma, pregnancy, cancer, elderly.
Normal D-Dimer effectively rules out thrombosis in low-risk patients.

low.text: Low/Normal D-Dimer: VTE unlikely (exclusion criteria met for low-risk patients).
<500 ng/mL with low pre-test probability excludes DVT/PE with >95% NPV.

remarks.text: <0.5 mg/L FEU = <500 ng/mL FEU (standard cutoff)
>0.5 mg/L: Elevated
<500 ng/mL FEU: Normal cutoff. Can exclude VTE in low-risk patients.
500-1000 ng/mL: Mild elevation. May indicate minor thrombosis, infection, or inflammation.
1000-2000 ng/mL: Moderate elevation. Suggests active thrombosis or DIC.
>2000 ng/mL: Significant elevation. DVT, PE, DIC, or severe infection likely.
<0.5 µg/mL FEU = <500 ng/mL FEU

description.text: Direct (conjugated) bilirubin is water-soluble and excreted in bile. Elevated in liver disease and bile duct obstruction.
Direktes (konjugiertes) Bilirubin ist wasserlöslich und wird über die Galle ausgeschieden.
High direct bilirubin: Hepatitis, cirrhosis, bile duct obstruction (stones, tumor), drug-induced liver injury.

high.text: Elevated direct bilirubin: Hepatocellular disease (hepatitis, cirrhosis), cholestasis (bile duct obstruction), drug-induced liver injury, Dubin-Johnson syndrome.

low.text: Low direct bilirubin: Normal finding. Not clinically significant.

remarks.text: Direct (conjugated) bilirubin <0.3 mg/dL is normal
>0.3 mg/dL: Elevated direct bilirubin. Suggests hepatic or post-hepatic (obstructive) jaundice.
>0.5 mg/dL with high total: Suggests bile duct obstruction or hepatitis.

description.text: eGFR (estimated GFR) is the best overall measure of kidney function. Calculated from creatinine using CKD-EPI 2021 equation.
Die eGFR ist der beste Gesamtmarker der Nierenfunktion und wird aus Kreatinin berechnet.
CKD-EPI 2021 equation is race-free and more accurate than MDRD, especially at higher GFR values.

high.text: High eGFR (>120): Usually normal in young healthy adults. May indicate hyperfiltration in early diabetes.

low.text: eGFR <60: Chronic kidney disease (CKD) if persistent >3 months. Check albuminuria for complete staging (G + A).
Rapid decline: >5 mL/min/1.73m² per year indicates rapid progression. Requires aggressive management.

remarks.text: G1: ≥90 mL/min/1.73m² (normal or high). Note: G1 alone does not indicate CKD unless other markers present.
G2: 60-89 (mildly decreased). May be normal for elderly. Not CKD unless other markers.
G3a: 45-59 (mildly to moderately decreased). CKD if persistent >3 months.
G3b: 30-44 (moderately to severely decreased). Monitor closely. Nephrology referral if progressive.
G4: 15-29 (severely decreased). Prepare for renal replacement therapy. Nephrology care essential.
G5: <15 (kidney failure). Dialysis or transplant usually required. Urgent nephrology referral.
Optimal ≥90 mL/min/1.73m² for longevity and healthy aging

description.text: Eosinophils are involved in allergic reactions and parasitic infections. Release histamine and other mediators.
Eosinophile sind an Allergien und Parasitenabwehr beteiligt.
High eosinophils: Allergies, asthma, parasitic infections. Very high: Hypereosinophilic syndrome.

high.text: Eosinophilia: Allergies (asthma, hay fever), parasitic infections, drug reactions, autoimmune disease, hypereosinophilic syndrome.

low.text: Eosinopenia: Rarely significant. May occur with acute bacterial infections, stress, steroid therapy.

remarks.text: Eosinophils 1-6% of total WBC per NHS guidelines
Mild eosinophilia 6-15%: Allergies, asthma, parasitic infection.
Moderate eosinophilia >15%: Parasitic infection, allergic disorders, drug reaction.
Absolute eosinophil count 0.0-0.5 ×10^9/L
>0.5: Eosinophilia. Allergies, parasites, autoimmune disease.

description.text: Erythrocytes (red blood cells) carry oxygen from lungs to tissues. Measured in millions per microliter.
Erythrozyten (rote Blutkörperchen) transportieren Sauerstoff vom Lungengewebe in alle Körperregionen.

high.text: High RBC (polycythemia): Dehydration, chronic hypoxia (COPD, high altitude), polycythemia vera, smoking.

low.text: Low RBC (anemia): Blood loss, hemolysis, bone marrow disorders, nutritional deficiencies (iron, B12, folate).

remarks.text: Male RBC 4.5-5.9 ×10^12/L per NHS guidelines
Female RBC 4.0-5.2 ×10^12/L per NHS guidelines

description.text: Ferritin is the most sensitive test for iron deficiency. Reflects iron stores. Note: Also an acute phase reactant (elevated in inflammation).
Ferritin ist der beste Marker für den Eisenstatus, aber auch ein Akute-Phase-Protein.
<15 ng/mL = depleted stores (iron deficiency). >1000 ng/mL = possible hemochromatosis. Inflammation falsely elevates ferritin.

high.text: High ferritin: Iron overload (hemochromatosis), inflammation, infection, liver disease, malignancy. Check TSAT to differentiate.

low.text: Low ferritin (<15): Iron deficiency (diagnostic). <30: Iron deficiency likely. Earliest marker of iron deficiency.

remarks.text: <15 ng/mL: Depleted iron stores. Diagnostic of iron deficiency per WHO.
15-30 ng/mL: Low iron stores. Iron deficiency likely even if Hb normal.
Male 30-300 ng/mL is normal per WHO
Female 15-150 ng/mL is normal per WHO
Optimal functional range 50-150 ng/mL. Associated with best outcomes.
150-1000 ng/mL: May indicate inflammation (acute phase reactant) or iron overload. Check CRP.
>1000 ng/mL: Iron overload. Hemochromatosis or severe inflammation. Evaluate urgently.
>2000 ng/mL: Definite iron overload. Hemochromatosis likely. Phlebotomy needed.

description.text: Folate (Vitamin B9) essential for DNA synthesis and cell division. Deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia.
Serum folate reflects recent intake; RBC folate reflects body stores over 2-3 months.
Critical in pregnancy: RBC folate >400 ng/mL (906 nmol/L) reduces neural tube defect risk. Women should take 400 μg/day before conception.

high.text: High folate: Usually supplementation. Rarely significant. Can mask B12 deficiency if B12 not checked.
Important: Always check B12 with folate. Folate can correct anemia but B12 deficiency neuropathy continues.

low.text: Low folate: Inadequate diet (destroyed by overcooking), malabsorption (celiac, tropical sprue), alcoholism, pregnancy, anticonvulsants.
Symptoms: Megaloblastic anemia, glossitis, fatigue. In pregnancy: Neural tube defects.

remarks.text: Normal serum folate >4.0 ng/mL (>9.1 nmol/L). Reflects recent intake.
2-4 ng/mL (4.5-9.1 nmol/L): Borderline. May indicate inadequate intake or early deficiency.
<2 ng/mL (<4.5 nmol/L): Deficiency. Associated with megaloblastic anemia.
>4 ng/mL: Adequate for pregnancy. RBC folate >400 ng/mL recommended to prevent neural tube defects.
Normal 9.1-45.3 nmol/L. Conversion: ng/mL × 2.266 = nmol/L
<6.8 nmol/L (<3 ng/mL): Deficiency
>4.0 μg/L = >4.0 ng/mL (same value, different units)

description.text: FT3 (Free T3) is the active form of thyroid hormone. Only 0.3% of total T3 is free (unbound to proteins).
FT3 ist das aktive Schilddrüsenhormon, das direkt auf den Stoffwechsel wirkt.
FT3 is rarely needed in initial evaluation - use TSH and FT4 first. FT3 useful for diagnosing T3 toxicosis.

high.text: High FT3: Indicates hyperthyroidism. T3 toxicosis = elevated FT3 with normal FT4 (5% of hyperthyroid cases).

low.text: Low FT3: Found in severe hypothyroidism, but also in euthyroid sick syndrome (non-thyroidal illness).

remarks.text: FT3 2.0-4.2 pg/mL is normal range (method-dependent). Active thyroid hormone.
Conversion: pg/mL × 1.54 = pmol/L

description.text: FT4 (Free T4) is the main thyroid hormone produced by the thyroid gland. Only 0.03% is free (unbound).
FT4 (Thyroxin) ist das Hauptschilddrüsenhormon und wird in aktives T3 umgewandelt.
FT4 together with TSH is the standard screening panel for thyroid function.

high.text: High FT4 with low TSH = primary hyperthyroidism (Graves disease, toxic nodule).
High FT4 with normal/high TSH = thyroid hormone resistance (rare).

low.text: Low FT4 with high TSH = primary hypothyroidism (most common pattern).
Low FT4 with low/normal TSH = central hypothyroidism (pituitary/hypothalamic).

remarks.text: FT4 0.8-1.7 ng/dL is normal range (method-dependent).
Conversion: ng/dL × 12.87 = pmol/L

description.text: Gamma globulin contains immunoglobulins (antibodies): IgG, IgA, IgM, IgD, IgE.
Polyclonal elevation: Chronic infections, inflammation, liver disease, autoimmune disorders.
Monoclonal elevation (M-spike): Multiple myeloma, Waldenström macroglobulinemia, MGUS, lymphoma.
Decreased: Immunodeficiency, nephrotic syndrome, protein-losing enteropathy.

high.text: High gamma (polyclonal): Chronic infections, liver disease, autoimmune disease, inflammation.
High gamma (monoclonal/M-spike): Multiple myeloma, MGUS, Waldenström macroglobulinemia, lymphoma. Requires immunofixation.

low.text: Low gamma: Immunodeficiency, nephrotic syndrome, protein-losing enteropathy, severe burns.

remarks.text: 11.1-18.8% of total protein
0.6-1.4 g/dL. Contains immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA, IgM).
1.4-2.5 g/dL: Polyclonal elevation. Chronic infections, liver disease, autoimmune disease.
>2.5 g/dL: Suspicious for monoclonal gammopathy. Check for M-spike (myeloma, MGUS).

description.text: GGT (Gamma-Glutamyltransferase) is most sensitive for liver disease, especially alcohol-related. Found in liver, kidney, pancreas.
GGT ist ein Leberenzym, das besonders bei Alkoholkonsum und Medikamenteneinnahme ansteigt.
GGT is the most sensitive but least specific liver enzyme. Elevated by alcohol, medications (phenytoin, warfarin), fatty liver.

high.text: Elevated GGT: Alcohol use (most common), fatty liver, medications (anticonvulsants, warfarin), cholestasis, liver disease.
>100 U/L: Suggests alcohol consumption even without other liver enzyme elevations.

low.text: Low GGT: Rarely clinically significant. May indicate B6 vitamin deficiency or hypothyroidism.

remarks.text: Male GGT 10-71 U/L per DGKL/VDGH 2005. Higher than females due to muscle mass.
Female GGT 6-42 U/L per DGKL/VDGH 2005
>100 U/L suggests alcohol use or medication effect. Check history.
Mild elevation 1-3× ULN. Common in alcohol use, fatty liver, medications.
Moderate elevation 3-10× ULN. Suggests significant liver disease or heavy alcohol use.
>10× ULN. Suggests alcoholic hepatitis, drug toxicity, or bile duct obstruction.

description.text: Glucose is the primary sugar found in blood and the body's main source of energy.
Glukose ist der wichtigste Energielieferant für den Körper.

high.text: Hyperglycemia: Indicates prediabetes or diabetes. Causes include insulin resistance, beta-cell dysfunction, stress, infection, medications (steroids).
Prediabetes (100-125 mg/dL fasting): Increased risk of diabetes and cardiovascular disease.

low.text: Hypoglycemia (<60 mg/dL): May indicate excess insulin, adrenal insufficiency, liver disease, or medication effects.

remarks.text: Fasting plasma glucose 60-100 mg/dL is normal per ADA 2024 Standards of Care
Fasting 100-125 mg/dL indicates prediabetes (impaired fasting glucose)
Fasting ≥126 mg/dL on two occasions indicates diabetes
Random glucose <140 mg/dL is normal
Random ≥200 mg/dL with symptoms suggests diabetes
Optimal range 70-85 mg/dL for longevity and metabolic health

description.text: HbA1c measures average blood glucose over the past 2-3 months. It reflects the percentage of hemoglobin coated with glucose.
HbA1c (Glykierter Hämoglobin) spiegelt die durchschnittliche Blutzuckerkonzentration der letzten 8-12 Wochen wider.
IFCC standardization ensures consistent results across laboratories worldwide.

high.text: Prediabetes (5.7-6.4%): 15-30% risk of developing diabetes within 5 years.
Diabetes (≥6.5%): Confirmed on repeat testing unless clear symptoms present.
Poor control (>9%): High risk of complications (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy).

low.text: HbA1c <4%: Rare, may indicate frequent hypoglycemia or hemoglobin variant.

remarks.text: HbA1c <5.7% is normal per ADA 2024
HbA1c 5.7-6.4% indicates prediabetes
HbA1c ≥6.5% indicates diabetes (confirmed on repeat)
ADA goal for most non-pregnant adults with diabetes: <7%
Optimal range 4.8-5.2% associated with longevity and reduced mortality
Excellent glycemic control: <6% without significant hypoglycemia

description.text: HBDH (α-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase) measures LDH isoenzymes LDH1 and LDH2, predominantly found in heart muscle.
Rises 8-10 hours after myocardial infarction, peaks at 2-4 days. Now largely replaced by troponin.
HBDH/LDH ratio helps differentiate cardiac (>0.9) from liver (<0.6) source of LDH elevation.
Still useful for late-presenting MI (>24h) when troponin may be declining.

high.text: High HBDH: Myocardial infarction (historical marker), hemolysis, muscle injury, renal disease.
HBDH/LDH ratio >0.9 suggests cardiac origin; <0.6 suggests liver origin.

low.text: Low HBDH: Rarely clinically significant.

remarks.text: <182 U/L at 37°C (Diasys). Reference range for adults.
<140 U/L at 25°C. Temperature-dependent reference range.
182-290 U/L: Mild elevation. May indicate myocardial injury or hemolysis.
>290 U/L: Significant elevation. Myocardial infarction, severe hemolysis, or muscle injury.
>180 U/L: Elevated. Consider MI if clinical presentation compatible. Check troponin.
<3.03 µkat/L at 37°C = <182 U/L
<2.33 µkat/L at 25°C = <140 U/L

description.text: HDL (high-density lipoprotein) transports cholesterol from tissues to liver. Higher levels are protective.
HDL-Cholesterin wird als 'gutes' Cholesterin bezeichnet, da es überschüssiges Cholesterin aus den Gefäßen transportiert.
Note: ESC/EAS 2019 does not recommend HDL as a target for therapy due to insufficient evidence from trials.

high.text: High HDL (≥60 mg/dL): Cardioprotective. However, very high HDL (>80-100) may not provide additional benefit per recent studies.

low.text: Low HDL: Associated with increased CVD risk. Causes include smoking, obesity, physical inactivity, diabetes, genetic factors.

remarks.text: HDL <40 mg/dL in males is low and constitutes a cardiovascular risk factor per ESC/EAS 2019
HDL <50 mg/dL in females is low and constitutes a cardiovascular risk factor per ESC/EAS 2019
HDL 40-60 mg/dL is considered acceptable
HDL ≥60 mg/dL is cardioprotective and associated with reduced CVD risk

description.text: Hematocrit is the percentage of blood volume occupied by red blood cells. Approximately 3× the hemoglobin value.
Der Hämatokrit gibt den Anteil der roten Blutkörperchen am Blutvolumen an.
Hematocrit is affected by plasma volume (dehydration increases, overhydration decreases) and RBC mass.

high.text: High hematocrit: Dehydration (hemoconcentration), polycythemia vera, COPD, high altitude, smoking.

low.text: Low hematocrit: Anemia (blood loss, hemolysis, nutritional deficiency), overhydration, pregnancy.

remarks.text: Male hematocrit 40-54% per NHS guidelines
Female hematocrit 36-48% per NHS guidelines
Male <41% suggests anemia (approximate)
Female <36% suggests anemia (approximate)
>55% may indicate dehydration or polycythemia

description.text: Hemoglobin is the protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen. Most important measure for anemia.
Hämoglobin ist das rote Blutfarbstoff und transportiert Sauerstoff.
WHO definition: Anemia = Hb <13 g/dL (men), <12 g/dL (women), <11 g/dL (pregnant).

high.text: High hemoglobin (polycythemia): Dehydration, COPD, high altitude, smoking, polycythemia vera.

low.text: Anemia: Blood loss, iron deficiency, B12/folate deficiency, chronic disease, hemolysis, bone marrow failure.
Symptoms of anemia: Fatigue, weakness, pallor, dyspnea, tachycardia.

remarks.text: Male hemoglobin 13.5-17.5 g/dL (WHO/NHS). <13 = anemia.
Female hemoglobin 12.0-16.0 g/dL (WHO/NHS). <12 = anemia.
Male <13 g/dL = anemia per WHO definition
Female <12 g/dL = anemia per WHO definition
<8 g/dL = severe anemia. May require transfusion.
<6.5 g/dL = critical, life-threatening anemia

description.text: Homocysteine is a sulfur-containing amino acid. Independent cardiovascular risk factor.
5 μmol/L increase = 20-30% higher CAD risk, 60% higher stroke risk.
Metabolized by B vitamins (folate, B6, B12). Elevated in deficiency of these vitamins.
Also elevated in: MTHFR mutation, renal failure, hypothyroidism, certain medications.

high.text: High homocysteine: B12/folate/B6 deficiency, MTHFR mutation, renal failure, hypothyroidism, smoking.
Associated with: Atherosclerosis, venous thrombosis, osteoporosis, cognitive decline.
Treat with: Folic acid, B12, B6 supplementation (reduces levels 20-25%).

low.text: Low homocysteine: Usually not clinically significant. May indicate malnutrition or over-supplementation.

remarks.text: Optimal: 5-10 μmol/L. Associated with lowest cardiovascular risk.
5-15 μmol/L: Standard reference range. Some use <12 as more optimal cutoff.
10-15 μmol/L: Borderline. May indicate mild B vitamin deficiency or increased risk.
15-30 μmol/L: Elevated (moderate hyperhomocysteinemia). Increased cardiovascular risk.
30-100 μmol/L: High (intermediate hyperhomocysteinemia). B vitamin deficiency likely.
>100 μmol/L: Severe hyperhomocysteinemia. Suggests homocystinuria or severe deficiency.
<30 μmol/L 4 hours after methionine load: Normal (functional test).

description.text: IgA is the second most abundant immunoglobulin (~15% of total). Found in mucosal surfaces (gut, respiratory tract, saliva, tears).
IgA protects mucous membranes from infection. Elevated in chronic infections and liver disease.
IgA deficiency occurs in 1 in 700 people and may be asymptomatic. Associated with recurrent sinopulmonary infections in some patients.

high.text: Elevated IgA: Chronic infections, liver disease (especially alcoholic cirrhosis), IgA myeloma, lymphoproliferative disorders.
Mild elevation may be non-specific. Significant elevation requires serum protein electrophoresis to exclude monoclonal gammopathy.

low.text: Low IgA: Selective IgA deficiency (1 in 700), protein-losing states (nephrotic syndrome, enteropathy), immunosuppression.
Check serum free light chains (SFLC) if IgA low to exclude light chain-only myeloma.

remarks.text: Adult IgA 0.8-3.0 g/L per OUH/DGKL. Age-related ranges in children.
3.0-4.0 g/L: Mild elevation. May be chronic inflammation or early monoclonal gammopathy.
4.0-6.0 g/L: Moderate elevation. Check serum electrophoresis for paraprotein.
>6.0 g/L: Significant elevation. Suggests IgA myeloma or severe polyclonal response. Urgent hematology referral.
<0.8 g/L: Low IgA. Check for immunodeficiency, protein loss, or check SFLC for light chain myeloma.
<0.1 g/L: Severe IgA deficiency. Occurs in 1 in 700 people. May not be symptomatic but beware of transfusion reactions.

description.text: IgE is the least abundant immunoglobulin (<0.2% of total). Mediates allergic reactions and defense against parasites.
IgE binds to mast cells and basophils. Cross-linking by allergen triggers histamine release - type I hypersensitivity.
Very high total IgE (>5000) usually indicates patient will have high specific IgEs and detectable allergies.

high.text: Elevated IgE: Atopic diseases (asthma, eczema, allergic rhinitis), parasitic infections, allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis, hyper IgE syndrome.
>5000 kU/L: Hyper IgE syndrome (Job syndrome), severe atopic dermatitis, or tropical parasitic infection.

low.text: Low IgE: Non-atopic individual, rarely significant. Some immunodeficiencies (ataxia-telangiectasia).
<20 kU/L suggests non-allergic status but does not rule out allergic disease completely.

remarks.text: Adult total IgE <100 kU/L. Very low levels (<20) suggest non-atopic status.
<20 kU/L: Very low, suggests non-atopic (non-allergic) individual.
100-200 kU/L: Mild elevation. May be mild atopy or parasitic infection.
200-500 kU/L: Moderate elevation. Suggests atopic disease (allergic rhinitis, asthma, eczema).
500-1000 kU/L: High elevation. Strongly suggests allergic disease. Check specific IgE.
1000-5000 kU/L: Very high. Severe atopic disease, parasitic infection, or allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis.
>5000 kU/L: Extreme elevation. Hyper IgE syndrome, severe parasitic infection, or atopic dermatitis.
30-700 kU/L: Omalizumab (anti-IgE) therapy candidate range. Requires specific IgE testing.

description.text: IgG is the most abundant immunoglobulin (~80% of total). Provides long-term immunity against bacterial and viral infections.
IgG is the only antibody class that crosses the placenta, providing passive immunity to newborns.
Has 4 subclasses (IgG1-4). IgG1 and IgG3 respond to protein antigens; IgG2 to polysaccharides; IgG4 to allergens.

high.text: Elevated IgG: Chronic infections (especially HIV), liver disease, autoimmune disease, IgG myeloma, lymphoproliferative disorders.
Polyclonal elevation: Broad increase in all immunoglobulins. Monoclonal: Single spike on electrophoresis suggests myeloma.

low.text: Low IgG (hypogammaglobulinemia): CVID, X-linked agammaglobulinemia, protein-losing states, nephrotic syndrome, immunosuppressants.
Recurrent infections, especially sinopulmonary and GI, suggest primary immunodeficiency.

remarks.text: Adult IgG 6.0-16.0 g/L per OUH/DGKL. Most abundant immunoglobulin (~80% of total).
16-20 g/L: Mild elevation. Common in chronic infections, inflammation, liver disease.
20-30 g/L: Moderate elevation. Check serum electrophoresis for paraprotein vs polyclonal response.
>30 g/L: Significant elevation. Suggests IgG myeloma or severe chronic antigenic stimulation. Urgent hematology referral.
4-6 g/L: Mild decrease. May be physiological variant or mild protein loss.
<4 g/L: Hypogammaglobulinemia. Increased infection risk. Evaluate for CVID or secondary causes.
<2 g/L: Severe hypogammaglobulinemia. High risk of serious infections. Requires immunology referral.

description.text: IgM is the first antibody produced in primary immune response (~5% of total). Pentameric structure makes it very effective at agglutination.
IgM is the largest antibody. It is the first to respond to infection and indicates recent or acute infection.
Mild IgM decrease is common in adults >60 years and usually has doubtful clinical significance.

high.text: Elevated IgM: Acute infections, Waldenström macroglobulinemia (IgM myeloma), primary biliary cholangitis, nephrotic syndrome.
Very high IgM suggests Waldenström macroglobulinemia, especially with hyperviscosity symptoms.

low.text: Low IgM: Immunodeficiency (rarely isolated), protein-losing states, immunosuppressants. Often seen with other low immunoglobulins.
Isolated low IgM in elderly often has doubtful significance.

remarks.text: Adult IgM 0.4-2.5 g/L per OUH/DGKL. First antibody produced in immune response (~5% of total).
2.5-3.5 g/L: Mild elevation. May be acute infection or early immune response.
3.5-5.0 g/L: Moderate elevation. Check serum electrophoresis for paraprotein.
>5.0 g/L: Significant elevation. Suggests IgM myeloma (Waldenström macroglobulinemia) or severe infection.
0.2-0.4 g/L: Mild decrease. Common in elderly (>60 years) and often of doubtful significance.
<0.2 g/L: Low IgM. Check for immunodeficiency, protein loss, or SFLC for light chain myeloma.

description.text: Immature granulocytes (IG) are young white blood cells (metamyelocytes, myelocytes, promyelocytes) normally found only in bone marrow.
Presence in peripheral blood indicates active bone marrow response to infection/inflammation.
IG elevation often precedes WBC elevation by 24-48 hours, making it an early sepsis marker.
Modern hematology analyzers (Sysmex, etc.) automatically measure IG% and absolute IG count.

high.text: High IG%: Bacterial infection (most common), sepsis, inflammation, corticosteroid use, bone marrow stimulation.
IG% >3% has >90% specificity for sepsis. IG% >0.5% is early warning sign.
More sensitive than band count (immature neutrophils) for detecting infection.

low.text: Low/absent IG: Normal finding. No clinical significance.

remarks.text: Optimal: 0-0.5%. Most healthy individuals have 0% or very low IG%.
Normal: 0-1%. Standard reference range. May see small increases with minor infections.
1-2%: Mild elevation. Common with infection, inflammation, or recovery from illness.
2-3%: Moderate elevation. Suggests active bacterial infection or significant inflammation.
>3%: Highly specific for sepsis. >90% specificity for sepsis diagnosis.
>5%: Severe elevation. Strong indicator of sepsis or serious bacterial infection.
Optimal absolute count: 0-0.03 ×10³/μL (or 0-30/μL)
>0.3 ×10³/μL: >90% specificity for sepsis.

description.text: Immature neutrophils (band cells) are young neutrophils released from bone marrow during infection.
Left shift refers to increased immature neutrophils in peripheral blood. Indicates active marrow response.
Less sensitive than immature granulocyte (IG) count but still useful marker of infection.

high.text: High band count: Bacterial infection, inflammation, tissue necrosis, corticosteroid use.
Marked left shift (>20%): Severe infection. May see toxic granulation, Dohle bodies in severe sepsis.

low.text: Low band count: Normal finding. No clinical significance.

remarks.text: <6% bands (immature neutrophils) of total WBC. Some labs use <10%.
6-10%: Mild left shift. Early sign of bacterial infection or inflammation.
10-20%: Moderate left shift. Active bacterial infection.
>20%: Marked left shift. Severe infection, may see toxic granulation/Dohle bodies.

description.text: Indirect (unconjugated) bilirubin is insoluble in water and bound to albumin. Elevated in hemolysis and Gilbert syndrome.
Indirektes (unkonjugiertes) Bilirubin ist fettlöslich und an Albumin gebunden.
High indirect bilirubin: Hemolysis (RBC breakdown), Gilbert syndrome (benign), Crigler-Najjar syndrome (rare).

high.text: Elevated indirect bilirubin: Hemolytic anemia (increased RBC breakdown), Gilbert syndrome (benign, common), Crigler-Najjar syndrome (rare, severe), neonatal jaundice.

low.text: Low indirect bilirubin: Normal finding. Not clinically significant.

remarks.text: Indirect (unconjugated) bilirubin <1.0 mg/dL is normal
>1.0 mg/dL: Elevated indirect bilirubin. Suggests pre-hepatic (hemolytic) or hepatic cause.
>1.5 mg/dL with normal direct: Suggests hemolysis or Gilbert syndrome.
>15 mg/dL in newborns: Risk of kernicterus. Phototherapy or exchange transfusion needed.

description.text: INR (International Normalized Ratio) is the standardized measure of blood clotting time. Used to monitor warfarin therapy.
INR ist die international standardisierte Messung der Blutgerinnungszeit.
Target INR on warfarin: 2.0-3.0 for most conditions, 2.5-3.5 for mechanical valves. >4.5 = high bleeding risk.

high.text: High INR: Warfarin overdose, liver disease, vitamin K deficiency, DIC. Bleeding risk increases significantly >4.5.

low.text: Low INR: Warfarin underdosing, high vitamin K intake. Increased thrombosis risk.

remarks.text: INR 0.85-1.15 is normal (not on anticoagulants).
INR 2.0-3.0: Therapeutic range for warfarin (AF, DVT, PE).
INR 2.5-3.5: Mechanical heart valves (higher intensity).
INR 3.0-4.5: Supratherapeutic. Increased bleeding risk. Dose adjustment needed.
INR >4.5: High bleeding risk. Hold warfarin. May need vitamin K.
INR >5.0: Critical. High bleeding risk. Urgent reversal with vitamin K.

description.text: Insulin is a hormone produced by beta cells in the pancreas. Regulates blood glucose by facilitating cellular uptake.
Fasting insulin is a marker of insulin sensitivity. Elevated levels indicate insulin resistance.
HOMA-IR (Homeostatic Model Assessment for Insulin Resistance) calculated from fasting glucose and insulin.

high.text: High insulin: Insulin resistance, type 2 diabetes, obesity, metabolic syndrome, Cushing syndrome, acromegaly.
Insulin resistance increases risk of cardiovascular disease, fatty liver, polycystic ovary syndrome.

low.text: Low insulin: Type 1 diabetes (autoimmune destruction of beta cells), pancreatic insufficiency, fasting/starvation.
Low insulin with high glucose indicates insulin deficiency (type 1 diabetes or advanced type 2).

remarks.text: 0.3-3.5 ng/mL equivalent to 2-25 µIU/mL
14-174 pmol/L equivalent to 2-25 µIU/mL
Fasting insulin 2-25 µIU/mL (or µU/mL). Best measured in morning after 8-12h fast.
Optimal: <15 µIU/mL. Levels >15 suggest insulin resistance.
15-25 µIU/mL: Borderline/suggestive of insulin resistance. Calculate HOMA-IR.
>25 µIU/mL: Insulin resistance likely. Associated with metabolic syndrome, type 2 diabetes risk.

description.text: Iodine is essential for thyroid hormone synthesis (T3 and T4). 70-80% of body iodine is in the thyroid gland.
Urinary iodine concentration (UIC) is the standard test for population iodine status.
Deficiency causes: Goiter, hypothyroidism, cretinism (severe deficiency in pregnancy), intellectual disability in children.
Global impact: 2 billion people worldwide have insufficient iodine intake. Iodized salt prevents deficiency.

high.text: High iodine: Excessive intake (seafood, supplements, iodinated contrast), can cause thyroid dysfunction (Jod-Basedow phenomenon).

low.text: Low iodine: Iodine deficiency disorders (IDD), goiter, hypothyroidism, cretinism. Common in areas without iodized salt.

remarks.text: 388-765 nmol/L = 49-97 µg/L (conversion factor: 1 µg/L = 7.88 nmol/L)
49-97 µg/L: Normal serum iodine (90% reference range). Essential for thyroid hormone synthesis.
100-199 µg/L: Adequate urinary iodine (school-age children, WHO criteria).
50-99 µg/L: Mild deficiency (urine). May affect thyroid function.
20-49 µg/L: Moderate deficiency. Associated with goiter.
<20 µg/L: Severe deficiency. High risk of cretinism in infants, hypothyroidism.
150-249 µg/L: Adequate for pregnant women (WHO).

description.text: Serum iron measures circulating iron bound to transferrin. Highly variable (diurnal, dietary).
Serumeisen ist das im Blut zirkulierende Eisen. Starken Schwankungen unterworfen.
Serum iron alone is poor indicator of iron status. Must be interpreted with ferritin and TSAT.

high.text: High iron: Iron overload (hemochromatosis), hepatitis, hemolysis, iron supplements.

low.text: Low iron: Iron deficiency (most common), chronic disease, infection, menstruation.

remarks.text: Serum iron 11-30 μmol/L (males), 9-27 μmol/L (females). High diurnal variation.
<11 μmol/L suggests iron deficiency. Check ferritin and TSAT.
>30 μmol/L may indicate iron overload. Check ferritin and TSAT.

description.text: TIBC measures the blood's capacity to bind iron with transferrin. Indirect measure of transferrin levels.
TIBC ist ein indirekter Marker für Transferrin und steigt bei Eisenmangel an.
High TIBC = iron deficiency. Low TIBC = iron overload or chronic disease. Often replaced by direct transferrin measurement.

high.text: Elevated TIBC: Iron deficiency (most common), oral contraceptives, pregnancy.

low.text: Low TIBC: Iron overload, chronic disease, inflammation, liver disease, malnutrition.

remarks.text: TIBC 250-400 µg/dL is normal. Reflects transferrin capacity to bind iron.
>400 µg/dL: Elevated TIBC. Suggests iron deficiency (more transferrin produced).
<250 µg/dL: Low TIBC. Suggests iron overload or chronic disease.

description.text: LDH (Lactate Dehydrogenase) is found in heart, liver, muscle, kidney, blood cells. Elevated in tissue damage and hemolysis.
LDH kommt in vielen Geweben vor und ist wenig spezifisch.
Has 5 isoenzymes (LDH-1 to LDH-5). LDH-1 high in heart/hemolysis, LDH-5 high in liver. Nonspecific but useful for monitoring.

high.text: Elevated LDH: Tissue damage (MI, liver disease, hemolysis, malignancy, muscle injury), hemolysis (in vitro or in vivo), severe infections.

low.text: Low LDH: Rarely significant. Genetic deficiency (very rare).

remarks.text: LDH 135-225 U/L is normal per DGKL. Less specific enzyme found in many tissues.
225-450 U/L: Mild elevation. Non-specific tissue injury.
>300 U/L with hemolysis: Check LDH-1/LDH-2 ratio and haptoglobin.
>450 U/L: Marked elevation. Significant tissue damage or hemolysis.

description.text: LDL (low-density lipoprotein) is the PRIMARY TARGET for lipid-lowering therapy per ESC/EAS 2019.
LDL transportiert Cholesterin zu den Geweben und ist der 'böse' Cholesterin - Hauptursache für Arteriosklerose.
Risk-based targets: Very high risk <55, High risk <70, Moderate <100, Low <116 mg/dL

high.text: Elevated LDL: Primary driver of atherosclerosis. Causes include diet high in saturated fat, genetics (FH), hypothyroidism, nephrotic syndrome.
LDL ≥190 mg/dL: May indicate familial hypercholesterolemia, requires genetic evaluation.

low.text: Low LDL (<50 mg/dL): Generally protective. If <20, verify no malabsorption or liver disease.

remarks.text: LDL <55 mg/dL for very high-risk patients (ESC/EAS 2019). Optional: <40 mg/dL if feasible without side effects.
LDL <70 mg/dL for high-risk patients (ESC/EAS 2019). Reduction ≥50% from baseline.
LDL <100 mg/dL for moderate-risk patients (ESC/EAS 2019)
LDL <116 mg/dL for low-risk patients (ESC/EAS 2019)
Optimal <70 mg/dL for primary prevention and longevity
LDL ≥160 mg/dL is high, requires lifestyle and/or pharmacological intervention

description.text: LDL/HDL ratio is a strong predictor of cardiovascular risk, sometimes better than individual lipid values.
Also known as Castelli Index. Ratio incorporates both 'bad' and 'good' cholesterol information.

high.text: High ratio: Indicates atherogenic lipid profile. Focus on lowering LDL and raising HDL.

remarks.text: LDL/HDL ratio <2.0 is optimal for cardiovascular health
LDL/HDL ratio <2.5 is considered normal
Ratio 2.5-3.5 indicates moderate CVD risk
Ratio >3.5 indicates increased CVD risk

description.text: Leukocytes (white blood cells) fight infection and protect against disease. Includes neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, basophils.
Leukozyten (weiße Blutkörperchen) sind Teil des Immunsystems und bekämpfen Infektionen.
Always check differential count to identify which WBC types are affected.

high.text: Leukocytosis: Bacterial infection, inflammation, stress, exercise, pregnancy, leukemia, corticosteroids.

low.text: Leukopenia: Viral infections, autoimmune disease, medications (chemotherapy, immunosuppressants), bone marrow failure, severe sepsis.

remarks.text: WBC 4.0-11.0 ×10^9/L is normal per NHS guidelines
Mild leukopenia 3.0-4.0: May be normal variant or early decrease.
Leukopenia <4.0: Viral infections, autoimmune, medications, bone marrow disorders.
Mild leukocytosis 11-15: Infection, inflammation, stress, exercise, pregnancy.
Leukocytosis >15: Significant infection, inflammation, leukemia. Check differential.
>30: Critical leukocytosis. Possible leukemia or severe infection. Urgent evaluation needed.
<1.5: Critical leukopenia. High infection risk. Neutropenia precautions.

description.text: Lipase is the preferred test for pancreatitis. More specific and stays elevated longer than amylase.
Lipase ist spezifischer für Pankreatitis als Amylase.
>3× ULN is diagnostic for pancreatitis (ACG guidelines). Remains elevated longer than amylase (7-14 days).

high.text: Elevated lipase: Acute pancreatitis (most specific), chronic pancreatitis, pancreatic cancer, intestinal obstruction, severe gastroenteritis.

low.text: Low lipase: Rarely significant. Chronic pancreatic insufficiency, cystic fibrosis.

remarks.text: Lipase 13-60 U/L is normal per DGKL. More specific for pancreatitis than amylase.
60-180 U/L: Mild elevation. May be non-specific.
>180 U/L (>3× ULN): Suggests acute pancreatitis per ACG guidelines.
>500 U/L: Severe pancreatitis. Correlate with clinical findings.

description.text: Lipoprotein(a) is a genetic cardiovascular risk factor independent of LDL cholesterol. 20% of population has elevated levels.
Lp(a) promotes atherosclerosis and blood clotting. High levels increase risk of heart attack and stroke.
NOT affected by diet or exercise. Levels are genetically determined and remain stable throughout life.
Screen once in lifetime. If elevated, treat other risk factors aggressively.

high.text: High Lp(a): Independent genetic risk factor for ASCVD. Cannot be lowered by lifestyle. Treat other risk factors aggressively.
>50 mg/dL or >125 nmol/L: High risk. Cascade screening of first-degree relatives recommended.

low.text: Normal Lp(a): <30 mg/dL or <75 nmol/L. Does not eliminate CV risk but is favorable finding.

remarks.text: <30 mg/dL (<75 nmol/L): Normal.
>50 mg/dL (>125 nmol/L): High cardiovascular risk threshold.
<75 nmol/L (<30 mg/dL): Normal. Low cardiovascular risk.
75-125 nmol/L (30-50 mg/dL): Intermediate risk.
125-200 nmol/L (50-80 mg/dL): High risk. Consider intensive risk factor modification.
>200 nmol/L (>80 mg/dL): Very high risk. Cascade screening of first-degree relatives recommended.

description.text: Lymphocytes include T cells, B cells, and NK cells. Responsible for adaptive immunity and viral defense.
Lymphozyten sind verantwortlich für die adaptive Immunantwort und Virusabwehr.
High lymphocytes: Viral infection. Low lymphocytes: Immunosuppression, HIV, severe sepsis.

high.text: Lymphocytosis: Viral infections (EBV, CMV, hepatitis), lymphocytic leukemia, lymphoma, hyperthyroidism.

low.text: Lymphopenia: HIV/AIDS, immunosuppressive medications, corticosteroids, radiation, severe infections, malnutrition.

remarks.text: Lymphocytes 20-45% of total WBC per NHS guidelines
>45%: Relative lymphocytosis. Viral infection, chronic inflammation.
<20%: Relative lymphopenia. Bacterial infection, stress, steroids.
Absolute lymphocyte count 1.0-4.0 ×10^9/L
>4.0: Lymphocytosis. Viral infection (EBV, CMV), chronic lymphocytic leukemia.
<1.0: Lymphopenia. HIV, immunosuppression, severe infection, malnutrition.
<0.5: Severe lymphopenia. High infection risk. HIV or immunosuppression likely.

description.text: Magnesium is essential for muscle and nerve function, heart rhythm, bone health, and energy production. Cofactor for 300+ enzymes.
Magnesium ist ein essentieller Kofaktor für über 300 Enzyme.
Low magnesium is common and often overlooked. Always check in refractory hypokalemia or hypocalcemia. Only 1% in serum.

high.text: Hypermagnesemia: Renal failure (most common), excessive supplementation, tumor lysis syndrome.

low.text: Hypomagnesemia: Diuretics (most common), alcoholism, malabsorption, diarrhea, proton pump inhibitors.

remarks.text: Magnesium 0.75-1.05 mmol/L is normal. Cofactor for 300+ enzymes.
Optimal 0.80-0.95 mmol/L. Important for potassium and calcium regulation.
Hypomagnesemia 0.60-0.75. Often asymptomatic. Check if refractory hypokalemia.
Severe hypomagnesemia <0.60. Arrhythmias, seizures, tetany. EMERGENCY.
Critical <0.50. LIFE-THREATENING. Severe arrhythmias, respiratory failure.
Hypermagnesemia >1.05. Renal failure (most common). Weakness, hypotension, bradycardia.

description.text: MCH (Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin) measures the average amount of hemoglobin per red blood cell.
MCH gibt die durchschnittliche Hämoglobinmenge pro Erythrozyt an.
MCH typically parallels MCV. Low MCH = hypochromic cells (pale), High MCH = hyperchromic cells.

high.text: High MCH: Macrocytic anemias (B12/folate deficiency), liver disease, hypothyroidism.

low.text: Low MCH: Iron deficiency (most common), thalassemia, anemia of chronic disease, sideroblastic anemia.

remarks.text: MCH 27-33 pg is normal. Amount of hemoglobin per red blood cell.
Low MCH (<27 pg): Hypochromic RBCs. Seen in iron deficiency, thalassemia.
High MCH (>33 pg): Hyperchromic RBCs. Seen in macrocytic anemias (B12/folate deficiency).

description.text: MCHC (Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration) measures the concentration of hemoglobin in red blood cells.
MCHC ist die durchschnittliche Hämoglobinkonzentration in den Erythrozyten.
MCHC is relatively constant (32-36 g/dL). Low = hypochromic, High = hyperchromic (spherocytes).

high.text: High MCHC: Hereditary spherocytosis, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, cold agglutinins, hemolysis.

low.text: Low MCHC: Iron deficiency (most common), thalassemia, anemia of chronic disease. RBCs appear pale (hypochromic).

remarks.text: MCHC 32-36 g/dL is normal. Concentration of hemoglobin in RBCs.
Low MCHC (<32 g/dL): Hypochromic cells. Iron deficiency, thalassemia. RBCs appear pale on smear.
High MCHC (>36 g/dL): Spherocytosis (hereditary or autoimmune), hemolysis, cold agglutinins. RBCs appear dark.

description.text: MCV (Mean Corpuscular Volume) measures the average size of red blood cells. Critical for classifying anemia.
MCV ist das mittlere Erythrozytenvolumen und dient zur Klassifikation von Anämien.
MCV classification: Microcytic (<80), Normocytic (80-100), Macrocytic (>100 fL). Guides further testing.

high.text: High MCV (macrocytic): B12/folate deficiency (check serum levels), liver disease, alcohol use, hypothyroidism, MDS, pregnancy, reticulocytosis.

low.text: Low MCV (microcytic): Iron deficiency (most common), thalassemia trait, anemia of chronic disease, sideroblastic anemia, lead poisoning.

remarks.text: Microcytic: <80 fL. Causes: Iron deficiency, thalassemia, anemia of chronic disease, sideroblastic anemia.
Normocytic: 80-100 fL. Normal, acute blood loss, early iron deficiency, chronic disease, hemolysis.
Macrocytic: >100 fL. Causes: B12/folate deficiency, liver disease, alcohol, hypothyroidism, MDS, medications (methotrexate, zidovudine).

description.text: MDRD (Modification of Diet in Renal Disease) estimates GFR from serum creatinine, age, sex, race.
LEGACY EQUATION: CKD-EPI 2021 (race-free) is preferred per KDIGO 2024.
MDRD tends to underestimate GFR in healthy individuals (>60 mL/min) and overestimate in elderly.
CKD staging: G1-G5 based on eGFR. CKD diagnosis requires eGFR <60 OR kidney damage markers for >3 months.

high.text: eGFR >90: Normal or high. Does not exclude CKD if albuminuria or structural abnormalities present.

low.text: eGFR 60-89: Mildly decreased. CKD only if other kidney damage markers present.
eGFR 45-59: G3a CKD. Mild-moderate decrease. Monitor annually.
eGFR 30-44: G3b CKD. Moderate-severe decrease. Monitor every 6 months.
eGFR 15-29: G4 CKD. Severe decrease. Refer to nephrology. Prepare for dialysis/transplant.
eGFR <15: G5 CKD. Kidney failure. Requires dialysis or transplant.

remarks.text: ≥90 mL/min/1.73m²: G1 (Normal or high). CKD if other markers present (albuminuria, etc.).
60-89: G2 (Mildly decreased). CKD if other markers present.
45-59: G3a (Mildly to moderately decreased). CKD confirmed.
30-44: G3b (Moderately to severely decreased). Monitor progression.
15-29: G4 (Severely decreased). Prepare for renal replacement therapy.
<15: G5 (Kidney failure). Dialysis or transplant required.
<60: CKD threshold. Confirm with albuminuria or other markers. Must persist >3 months.

description.text: Monocytes are phagocytic cells that become macrophages in tissues. Involved in chronic inflammation.
Monozyten wandern in das Gewebe und werden zu Makrophagen.
High monocytes: Chronic inflammation, recovery phase of infection, autoimmune disease.

high.text: Monocytosis: Chronic inflammation (TB, sarcoidosis), autoimmune disease, recovery from acute infection, chronic myelomonocytic leukemia.

low.text: Monocytopenia: Rarely significant. May occur with hairy cell leukemia, steroid therapy.

remarks.text: Monocytes 2-10% of total WBC per NHS guidelines
>10%: Monocytosis. Chronic inflammation, recovery from infection.
Absolute monocyte count 0.2-1.0 ×10^9/L
>1.0: Monocytosis. Chronic inflammation, autoimmune disease, leukemia.

description.text: Neutrophils are the most abundant white blood cells. First responders to bacterial infection and inflammation.
Neutrophile Granulozyten bekämpfen vor allem bakterielle Infektionen.
High neutrophils: Bacterial infection. Low neutrophils: Viral infection, bone marrow suppression, autoimmune.

high.text: Neutrophilia: Bacterial infection, inflammation, stress, exercise, corticosteroids, smoking.

low.text: Neutropenia: Viral infections, medications (chemotherapy), bone marrow disorders, autoimmune (SLE), B12/folate deficiency.

remarks.text: Neutrophils 40-75% of total WBC per NHS guidelines
>75%: Neutrophilia. Bacterial infection, inflammation, stress, steroids.
<40%: Neutropenia or relative lymphocytosis. Check absolute count.
Absolute neutrophil count 2.0-7.5 ×10^9/L
Mild neutropenia 1.0-2.0: Monitor, increased infection risk.
Moderate neutropenia 0.5-1.0: Significant infection risk.
Severe neutropenia <0.5: High infection risk. Neutropenic precautions.
>7.5: Neutrophilia. Bacterial infection, inflammation, stress, corticosteroids.

description.text: Cardiac troponin I is the gold standard biomarker for myocardial infarction diagnosis. Highly specific for cardiac injury.
High-sensitivity assays can detect troponin in >50% of healthy individuals.
Serial testing essential - delta troponin >50% within 3-6 hours suggests acute MI.
Elevated in: MI, myocarditis, heart failure, PE, renal failure, sepsis.

high.text: High troponin: Acute MI (most critical), myocarditis, heart failure, pulmonary embolism, renal failure, sepsis.
Trend more important than absolute value. Rising levels suggest ongoing injury.

low.text: Low/undetectable troponin: Normal. MI unlikely if symptoms >6 hours.

remarks.text: <26 ng/L: Normal (99th percentile female, Abbott assay). Values vary by assay.
<34 ng/L: 99th percentile male (Abbott hs-cTnI).
26-52 ng/L: Elevated. Myocardial injury likely. Rule out acute MI with serial testing.
>52 ng/L: Highly elevated. Strongly suggests acute myocardial infarction.
<5 ng/L: Very low/undetectable. MI unlikely (LODx3 rule-out strategy).
<26 pg/mL = <26 ng/L (equivalent units)

description.text: Phosphate is essential for bone mineralization, energy metabolism (ATP), and cellular signaling. Regulated by PTH and vitamin D.
Phosphat ist wichtig für Knochen und Energiestoffwechsel.
Low phosphate common in refeeding syndrome, alcoholism, DKA recovery. High phosphate in CKD and hypoparathyroidism.

high.text: Hyperphosphatemia: Chronic kidney disease (most common), hypoparathyroidism, vitamin D excess, tumor lysis syndrome.

low.text: Hypophosphatemia: Refeeding syndrome, alcoholism, malabsorption, hyperparathyroidism, DKA recovery, diuretics.

remarks.text: Phosphate 0.80-1.45 mmol/L (adults). Essential for bone health and energy metabolism.
Hypophosphatemia 0.50-0.80. Common in hospitalized patients. Check if refeeding syndrome risk.
Severe hypophosphatemia <0.50. Muscle weakness, hemolysis, rhabdomyolysis. EMERGENCY.
Hyperphosphatemia >1.45. Chronic kidney disease (most common). Check PTH.

description.text: Potassium is the primary intracellular cation. Critical for cardiac rhythm, nerve conduction, and muscle contraction.
Kalium ist das wichtigste Intracellular-Kation und essentiell für Herzmuskelfunktion.
Hyperkalemia >6.5 mmol/L can cause fatal arrhythmias. Hypokalemia <2.5 can cause paralysis and rhabdomyolysis.

high.text: Hyperkalemia: Renal failure (most common), medications (ACE inhibitors, spironolactone, NSAIDs), hemolysis, acidosis, tissue breakdown.

low.text: Hypokalemia: Diuretics (most common), vomiting/diarrhea, alkalosis, magnesium deficiency, insulin therapy.

remarks.text: Potassium 3.5-5.0 mmol/L is normal. Critical for cardiac function.
Optimal 4.0-4.5 mmol/L for cardiovascular health.
Mild hypokalemia 3.0-3.5. Usually asymptomatic but requires monitoring.
Hypokalemia 2.5-3.0. Muscle weakness, arrhythmias possible. Urgent replacement needed.
Severe hypokalemia <2.5. LIFE-THREATENING. Paralysis, rhabdomyolysis, VT/VF risk.
Mild hyperkalemia 5.0-5.5. Check for hemolysis. Monitor closely.
Hyperkalemia 5.5-6.0. Cardiac risk. Check ECG. Treatment needed.
Moderate hyperkalemia 6.0-6.5. High cardiac risk. Immediate ECG and treatment.
Severe hyperkalemia >6.5. LIFE-THREATENING. Cardiac arrest risk. EMERGENCY treatment.
>7.0: CRITICAL. Immediate calcium gluconate, insulin/glucose, dialysis if indicated.

description.text: Procalcitonin (PCT) is a precursor to calcitonin. Rises rapidly (3-6 hours) in bacterial infections.
Excellent biomarker for bacterial sepsis vs viral infection. More specific than CRP or WBC.
Guides antibiotic stewardship: low levels discourage antibiotics, high levels encourage them.
Half-life ~24 hours. Levels fall rapidly with effective antibiotic therapy.

high.text: High PCT: Bacterial infection, sepsis, severe inflammation. Levels >0.5 ng/mL suggest bacterial origin.
>2 ng/mL: High probability of sepsis. >10 ng/mL: Severe sepsis/shock likely.

low.text: Low PCT (<0.1): Viral infection, non-infectious inflammation, or no infection. Antibiotics likely not needed.

remarks.text: <0.1 ng/mL: Normal. Sepsis unlikely.
0.1-0.25 ng/mL: Low likelihood of bacterial infection. Antibiotics discouraged.
0.25-0.5 ng/mL: Intermediate risk. Clinical correlation required.
0.5-2.0 ng/mL: High risk of sepsis. Antibiotics strongly encouraged.
2-10 ng/mL: Severe sepsis/septic shock likely.
>10 ng/mL: Critical. Severe bacterial infection or sepsis almost certain.
<0.1 µg/L = <0.1 ng/mL (equivalent units)
>0.5 µg/L: Sepsis threshold

description.text: PSA is a glycoprotein produced by prostate epithelial cells. Used for prostate cancer screening and monitoring.
2023 AUA Guidelines emphasize SHARED DECISION-MAKING. Not all men should be screened. Discuss risks/benefits.
Many false positives - elevated by benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), prostatitis, recent ejaculation, digital rectal exam.

high.text: High PSA: Prostate cancer, BPH (most common cause of elevation), prostatitis, recent ejaculation, prostate instrumentation.
PSA velocity >0.75 ng/mL/year suspicious even if total PSA normal. Free PSA <25% suggests cancer when total PSA 4-10.

low.text: Low PSA: Normal finding. However, some prostate cancers (especially aggressive) may not elevate PSA significantly.
PSA <4 ng/mL does not rule out cancer - 15% of men with PSA 2.5-4 have prostate cancer.

remarks.text: Age 40-49: <2.5 ng/mL. AUA recommends shared decision-making for screening.
Age 50-59: <3.5 ng/mL. PSA velocity >0.75 ng/mL/year is suspicious.
Age 60-69: <4.5 ng/mL. This age group has strongest evidence for screening benefit.
Age >70: <6.5 ng/mL. Screening benefit less clear in this age group.
4-10 ng/mL: Grey zone. 25% chance of cancer. Consider free PSA, PSA density, or MRI before biopsy.
>10 ng/mL: Highly suspicious for prostate cancer. >50% chance of malignancy.
>20 ng/mL: Very high. Strongly suggests prostate cancer; may indicate advanced disease.

description.text: PTT (Partial Thromboplastin Time) measures the intrinsic coagulation pathway. Used to monitor heparin therapy.
PTT misst die intrinsische Gerinnungskaskade und wird zur Heparin-Kontrolle verwendet.
PTT therapeutic range on heparin: 1.5-2.5× normal (usually 60-80 seconds). Used less with low molecular weight heparin.

high.text: Prolonged PTT: Heparin therapy, hemophilia (Factor VIII or IX deficiency), von Willebrand disease, liver disease, DIC.

low.text: Shortened PTT: Rare. May indicate hypercoagulable state, early DIC, or specimen error.

remarks.text: PTT 25-35 seconds is normal. Measures intrinsic coagulation pathway.
PTT 60-80s: Therapeutic range for unfractionated heparin (1.5-2.5× normal).
PTT 35-45s: Mildly prolonged. May be normal variant or early heparin effect.
PTT 45-60s: Prolonged. Heparin therapy, coagulation factor deficiency.
PTT >80s: Critical bleeding risk. High heparin dose or severe coagulopathy.

description.text: Quick test (Prothrombin Time/PT) measures the extrinsic coagulation pathway. Used with INR for warfarin monitoring.
Quick-Test misst die extrinsische Blutgerinnung.
Decreased in vitamin K deficiency, liver disease, warfarin therapy. Less commonly used than INR.

high.text: Quick >120%: Rare. Hemoconcentration, early stages of DIC.

low.text: Quick <70%: Vitamin K deficiency, liver disease, warfarin, DIC, factor VII deficiency.

remarks.text: Quick 70-120% is normal. Measures extrinsic coagulation pathway.
<70%: Prolonged coagulation. Vitamin K deficiency, liver disease, warfarin therapy.
<50%: Critical bleeding risk. Urgent evaluation needed.

description.text: RDW (Red Cell Distribution Width) measures the variation in red blood cell size (anisocytosis). Higher RDW = more size variation.
Die Erythrozytenverteilungsbreite (RDW) gibt die Variation der Erythrozytengröße an.
RDW is often the first indicator of iron deficiency, even before anemia develops. Also elevated in B12/folate deficiency, hemolysis.

high.text: High RDW: Iron deficiency (earliest sign), B12/folate deficiency, hemolysis, recent transfusion, sideroblastic anemia, myelodysplasia.
RDW + Low MCV: Iron deficiency or thalassemia. RDW + High MCV: B12/folate deficiency.

low.text: Low RDW: Rarely clinically significant. May indicate uniform RBCs (chronic transfusion, some hemoglobinopathies).

remarks.text: RDW 11.5-14.5% is normal. Measures variation in RBC size (anisocytosis).
RDW 14.5-18%: Mild elevation. Early iron deficiency, mixed deficiency, hemolysis.
RDW 18-22%: Moderate elevation. Significant anisocytosis. Iron deficiency, B12/folate deficiency, hemolysis.
RDW >22%: Severe elevation. Marked anisocytosis. Severe deficiency states, transfusion reaction, sideroblastic anemia.

description.text: Rheumatoid factor (RF) is an autoantibody directed against the Fc portion of IgG. IgM isotype most common.
Present in 80% of RA patients within 18 months of diagnosis. 20% of RA patients remain RF-negative.
NOT specific for RA. Also elevated in: Sjögren syndrome, SLE, chronic infections, endocarditis, aging.
ACR 2010 criteria use RF titer for diagnosis scoring: Low positive (15-39 IU/mL) = 2 points, High positive (>40) = 3 points.

high.text: High RF: Rheumatoid arthritis (most common), Sjögren syndrome, SLE, chronic infections (endocarditis, hepatitis), aging, cryoglobulinemia.
Higher titers (>40 IU/mL) associated with more aggressive RA, extra-articular manifestations, poorer prognosis.

low.text: Low/negative RF: Does NOT rule out RA. 20% of RA patients are seronegative (especially early disease).
If RF negative but RA suspected, test anti-CCP (anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide) antibodies - more specific.

remarks.text: <20 IE/mL = <20 IU/mL (equivalent units)
<15 IU/mL: Normal/negative. RF is an autoantibody against IgG Fc portion.
<20 IU/mL: Alternative normal cutoff (Cleveland Clinic, HSS). Some labs use this threshold.
15-20 IU/mL: Borderline/low positive. May be clinically insignificant or early disease.
15-39 IU/mL: Low positive. ACR 2010 criteria score = 2. Requires clinical correlation.
