>40 IU/mL: High positive. ACR 2010 criteria score = 3. Associated with more aggressive RA.
Titer <1:80 considered negative. Alternative reporting method.
<20 U/mL = <20 IU/mL (equivalent units)

description.text: Selenium is an essential trace mineral for thyroid hormone metabolism (T4 to T3 conversion) and antioxidant defense.
Deficiency causes: Keshan disease (cardiomyopathy), Kashin-Beck disease (osteoarthritis), hypothyroidism.
Sources: Brazil nuts, seafood, meat, grains. RDA: 55-70 µg/day for adults.
Toxicity (selenosis): >400 µg/day intake causes hair loss, nail changes, garlic breath odor.

high.text: High selenium: Excessive supplementation, occupational exposure, selenosis (toxicity).

low.text: Low selenium: Poor dietary intake, malabsorption, Keshan disease risk, Kashin-Beck disease, hypothyroidism.

remarks.text: 70-150 µg/L: Normal adult range (ARUP Labs). Essential for thyroid function and antioxidant defense.
<70 µg/L: Selenium deficiency. Associated with Keshan disease (cardiomyopathy) and Kashin-Beck disease.
150-300 µg/L: Elevated. Monitor for selenosis symptoms (hair/nail loss, garlic breath, GI upset).
>300 µg/L: Selenium toxicity (selenosis). Requires immediate medical attention.
0.9-1.9 µmol/L = 70-150 µg/L (conversion factor: 1 µmol/L = 78.96 µg/L)

description.text: Sodium is the primary extracellular cation. Regulates fluid balance, blood pressure, and osmotic pressure.
Natrium ist das wichtigste Extracellular-Kation und reguliert den Wasserhaushalt.
Hyponatremia: Check glucose (pseudohyponatremia if very high), assess volume status. Hypernatremia: Always indicates dehydration.

high.text: Hypernatremia: Dehydration (most common), diabetes insipidus, excessive salt intake, hyperaldosteronism.

low.text: Hyponatremia: SIADH (most common), heart failure, cirrhosis, hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, polydipsia.

remarks.text: Sodium 136-145 mmol/L is normal. Tight regulation essential for fluid balance.
Optimal 138-142 mmol/L.
Mild hyponatremia 130-136. Usually asymptomatic. Common in hospitalized patients.
Hyponatremia 125-130. Nausea, headache, malaise. Requires evaluation.
Severe hyponatremia <125. Confusion, seizures, cerebral edema. CORRECT SLOWLY.
Critical <120. LIFE-THREATENING. Severe neurological symptoms. EMERGENCY.
Hypernatremia 145-150. Thirst, weakness. Usually dehydration.
Severe hypernatremia >150. Confusion, seizures. Usually severe dehydration or diabetes insipidus.
Critical >160. LIFE-THREATENING. Severe neurological damage risk. EMERGENCY.

description.text: Testosterone is the primary male sex hormone (androgen). Also present in females in smaller amounts.
In males: Regulates libido, bone mass, fat distribution, muscle mass/strength, sperm production.
Shows diurnal variation: highest in morning (7-10 AM), decreases throughout day.
Low in males: Hypogonadism (primary or secondary), infertility, erectile dysfunction.
High in females: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), hirsutism, virilization.

high.text: High testosterone (males): Uncommon. May indicate androgen resistance or tumor.
High testosterone (females): PCOS, congenital adrenal hyperplasia, androgen-secreting tumor.

low.text: Low testosterone (males): Hypogonadism (primary or secondary), aging, chronic illness, malnutrition, medications (opioids, steroids).
Low testosterone (females): Menopause, pituitary disorders, adrenal insufficiency.

remarks.text: 300-1000 ng/dL: Normal male range (MedlinePlus). Best measured 7-10 AM due to diurnal variation.
15-70 ng/dL: Normal female range.
<300 ng/dL: Testosterone deficiency threshold per AUA Guidelines. Confirm with second morning level.
450-600 ng/dL: Target range for testosterone replacement therapy (middle tertile).
10-35 nmol/L = 300-1000 ng/dL (male)
0.5-2.4 nmol/L = 15-70 ng/dL (female)

description.text: Thrombocytes (platelets) are cell fragments that form clots to stop bleeding. Critical for hemostasis.
Thrombozyten (Blutplättchen) sind für die Blutgerinnung wichtig.
Low platelets = bleeding risk. High platelets = thrombosis risk.

high.text: Thrombocytosis: Infection, inflammation, iron deficiency, essential thrombocythemia, myeloproliferative disorders.

low.text: Thrombocytopenia: Viral infections, autoimmune (ITP), medications, bone marrow disorders, DIC, TTP/HUS, hypersplenism.

remarks.text: Platelets 150-400 ×10^9/L is normal per NHS guidelines
Mild thrombocytopenia 100-150: Monitor, may not require treatment if stable.
Thrombocytopenia <150: Bleeding risk increases as count drops. Check for causes.
Severe thrombocytopenia <50: High bleeding risk. Avoid trauma, procedures.
Critical <20: Very high bleeding risk. Spontaneous bleeding possible. Urgent treatment.
Mild thrombocytosis 400-500: Reactive (inflammation, iron deficiency), or essential thrombocythemia.
Thrombocytosis >500: Reactive (infection, inflammation), essential thrombocythemia, other myeloproliferative disorder.

description.text: Total bilirubin is the sum of direct (conjugated) and indirect (unconjugated) bilirubin. Elevated in liver disease and hemolysis.
Bilirubin ist ein Abbauprodukt des Hämoglobins und wird über die Leber ausgeschieden.
>2-3 mg/dL causes yellowing of sclera (eyes), >5 mg/dL causes skin jaundice.

high.text: Elevated total bilirubin: Liver disease (hepatitis, cirrhosis), hemolysis (high indirect), bile duct obstruction (high direct), Gilbert syndrome (mild, indirect).

low.text: Low bilirubin: Rarely significant. May indicate certain medications or hyperthyroidism.

remarks.text: Total bilirubin 0.1-1.2 mg/dL is normal
1.2-3.0 mg/dL: Mild elevation. Jaundice may not be visible.
2-5 mg/dL: Scleral icterus visible (yellowing of eyes).
5-20 mg/dL: Clinical jaundice (skin yellowing). Requires evaluation.
>20 mg/dL: Severe hyperbilirubinemia. Risk of complications. Urgent evaluation.

description.text: Total cholesterol includes HDL, LDL, and VLDL. It is a starting point for assessment but not the primary target.
Cholesterin ist ein wichtiger Baustein von Zellmembranen und wird zur Herstellung von Hormonen benötigt.

high.text: High total cholesterol: Risk factor for atherosclerosis, but must be evaluated with HDL/LDL levels.
Primary causes: Diet, genetics (familial hypercholesterolemia), hypothyroidism, nephrotic syndrome.

remarks.text: Total cholesterol <200 mg/dL is desirable per ESC/EAS 2019
200-239 mg/dL is borderline high
≥240 mg/dL is high total cholesterol
Optimal <180 mg/dL for cardiovascular health

description.text: Total protein measures all proteins in blood: albumin (55-60%) and globulins (40-45%).
Albumin maintains oncotic pressure; globulins include immunoglobulins and transport proteins.
High total protein: Dehydration, multiple myeloma, chronic inflammation.
Low total protein: Liver disease, kidney disease (nephrotic syndrome), malnutrition, burns.

high.text: High total protein: Dehydration (most common), multiple myeloma, Waldenström macroglobulinemia, chronic inflammatory states.
>8.5 g/dL: Rule out paraproteinemia (myeloma). Check serum protein electrophoresis.

low.text: Low total protein: Liver disease (decreased synthesis), kidney disease (nephrotic syndrome - protein loss), malnutrition, burns, protein-losing enteropathy.
<4.5 g/dL: Severe hypoproteinemia. Edema likely. Requires urgent evaluation.

remarks.text: 6.3-7.9 g/dL: Normal for ≥1 year (Mayo Clinic).
5.5-6.3 g/dL: Mild decrease. Early malnutrition or mild liver disease.
4.5-5.5 g/dL: Moderate decrease. Significant liver or kidney disease.
<4.5 g/dL: Severe hypoproteinemia. High risk of edema.
7.9-8.5 g/dL: Mild elevation. Usually dehydration or paraproteinemia.
>8.5 g/dL: Significant elevation. Multiple myeloma, Waldenström macroglobulinemia, or severe dehydration.
63-79 g/L = 6.3-7.9 g/dL (SI units)
>85 g/L = >8.5 g/dL (monoclonal gammopathy suspected)

description.text: Transferrin is the iron transport protein. Binds and transports iron in blood. Inverse relationship with iron stores.
Transferrin ist das Transportprotein für Eisen im Blut.
High transferrin suggests iron deficiency (body trying to absorb more iron). Low transferrin suggests iron overload or chronic disease.

high.text: High transferrin: Iron deficiency (compensatory increase), pregnancy, oral contraceptives.

low.text: Low transferrin: Iron overload, chronic disease, inflammation, liver disease, malnutrition.

remarks.text: Transferrin 2.0-3.6 g/L (200-360 mg/dL). Iron transport protein.
>3.6 g/L: Iron deficiency (liver produces more transferrin to bind available iron).
<2.0 g/L: Iron overload, chronic disease, malnutrition, liver disease.

description.text: Transferrin Saturation (TSAT) is the percentage of transferrin binding sites occupied by iron. Best indicator of iron availability to tissues.
Transferrinsättigung zeigt den Prozentsatz des mit Eisen besetzten Transferrins an.
<20% suggests iron deficiency. >50% suggests iron overload. Most reliable single test for iron status.

high.text: High TSAT (>50%): Iron overload (hemochromatosis), excessive iron supplementation, hemolysis.

low.text: Low TSAT (<16%): Iron deficiency. Insufficient iron for erythropoiesis.

remarks.text: <16%: Iron deficiency. Insufficient iron delivery to tissues.
20-50%: Normal transferrin saturation. Optimal iron delivery.
>50%: Elevated. May indicate iron overload. Check ferritin.
>45%: Screen for hemochromatosis if ferritin also elevated.

description.text: Triglycerides are the main form of fat storage. Elevated levels increase cardiovascular risk and pancreatitis risk.
Triglyceride sind die Hauptform gespeicherter Fette im Körper.
Note: Fasting 12-14 hours essential for accurate triglyceride measurement.

high.text: Elevated triglycerides: Associated with obesity, metabolic syndrome, diabetes, hypothyroidism, renal disease, excess alcohol.
Very high (≥500 mg/dL): Risk of acute pancreatitis. Requires immediate treatment (fibrate, omega-3).

low.text: Low triglycerides (<50 mg/dL): Usually not clinically significant. May indicate malnutrition or hyperthyroidism.

remarks.text: Triglycerides <150 mg/dL (<1.7 mmol/L) is normal per ESC/EAS 2019
150-199 mg/dL is borderline high. Lifestyle intervention recommended.
200-499 mg/dL is high. Evaluate secondary causes (diabetes, hypothyroidism, renal disease, medications).
≥500 mg/dL is very high. Risk of pancreatitis. Requires immediate intervention.
>880 mg/dL (>10 mmol/L): Very high risk of acute pancreatitis
Optimal <100 mg/dL for metabolic health

description.text: Cardiac troponin I is the gold standard biomarker for myocardial infarction diagnosis. Highly specific for cardiac injury.
High-sensitivity assays can detect troponin in >50% of healthy individuals.
Serial testing essential - delta troponin >50% within 3-6 hours suggests acute MI.
Elevated in: MI, myocarditis, heart failure, PE, renal failure, sepsis.

high.text: High troponin: Acute MI (most critical), myocarditis, heart failure, pulmonary embolism, renal failure, sepsis.
Trend more important than absolute value. Rising levels suggest ongoing injury.

low.text: Low/undetectable troponin: Normal. MI unlikely if symptoms >6 hours.

remarks.text: <26 ng/L: Normal (99th percentile female, Abbott assay). Values vary by assay.
<34 ng/L: 99th percentile male (Abbott hs-cTnI).
26-52 ng/L: Elevated. Myocardial injury likely. Rule out acute MI with serial testing.
>52 ng/L: Highly elevated. Strongly suggests acute myocardial infarction.
<5 ng/L: Very low/undetectable. MI unlikely (LODx3 rule-out strategy).
<26 pg/mL = <26 ng/L (equivalent units)

description.text: High-sensitivity cardiac troponin T is used for early diagnosis of acute myocardial infarction.
Roche hs-cTnT assay lower limit of detection: 3 ng/L (LoB), 5 ng/L (LoD).
Serial testing at 0 and 3-6 hours recommended for MI diagnosis.
hs-cTnT slightly elevated in chronic kidney disease (reduced clearance).

high.text: High troponin T: Acute MI, myocarditis, heart failure, pulmonary embolism, renal failure, sepsis.
hs-cTnT >52 ng/L at presentation: High probability of acute MI (0/1-hour algorithm).

low.text: Low/undetectable: MI unlikely. <5 ng/L can rule out MI with >99% NPV.

remarks.text: <14 ng/L: Normal (99th percentile). Detectable in >50% of healthy population.
3-14 ng/L: Detectable but normal. Seen in >50% of healthy individuals.
14-52 ng/L: Mild elevation. Myocardial injury present. Serial testing to rule out MI.
>52 ng/L: Abnormal threshold. High probability of acute MI per ESC guidelines.
<5 ng/L: Rule-out MI at presentation (0/1-hour algorithm).
<14 pg/mL = <14 ng/L (equivalent units)

description.text: TSH (Thyroid Stimulating Hormone) is the most sensitive test for thyroid function. It rises when thyroid hormone is low and falls when thyroid hormone is high.
TSH ist das wichtigste Screening-Verfahren zur Beurteilung der Schilddrüsenfunktion.
TSH has a circadian rhythm - highest in morning, lowest in evening. Morning testing recommended.

high.text: High TSH (>4.0): Indicates hypothyroidism. Causes include Hashimoto thyroiditis, iodine deficiency, post-ablative therapy, medications (amiodarone, lithium).
Subclinical hypothyroidism (TSH 4-10): Consider treatment if symptomatic, TPO antibodies positive, or goiter present.

low.text: Low TSH (<0.4): Indicates hyperthyroidism or excess thyroid hormone replacement. Causes include Graves disease, toxic nodules, excess levothyroxine.
Subclinical hyperthyroidism (TSH 0.1-0.4): May cause atrial fibrillation and osteoporosis risk.

remarks.text: TSH 0.4-4.0 mIU/L is euthyroid (normal) per ATA 2020. Some labs use upper limit 4.5.
Optimal functional range 0.5-2.5 mIU/L - associated with better symptom resolution
TSH 0.1-0.4 with normal FT4 indicates subclinical hyperthyroidism
TSH 4.0-10.0 with normal FT4 indicates subclinical hypothyroidism
TSH <0.1 with elevated FT4/FT3 indicates overt hyperthyroidism
TSH >10 with low FT4 indicates overt hypothyroidism
1st trimester TSH target 0.1-2.5 mIU/L per ATA 2020
2nd trimester TSH target 0.2-3.0 mIU/L per ATA 2020
3rd trimester TSH target 0.3-3.0 mIU/L per ATA 2020

description.text: Urea (blood urea nitrogen/BUN) is a waste product of protein metabolism. Less accurate than creatinine or eGFR for kidney function.
Harnstoff (BUN) ist ein Stoffwechselprodukt des Eiweißabbaus und wird über die Niere ausgeschieden.
Urea is affected by protein intake, hydration, and catabolic state, making it less reliable than creatinine.

high.text: Elevated urea: Kidney disease (reduced clearance), dehydration (prerenal), high protein diet, catabolic states, upper GI bleeding.
BUN/Creatinine ratio >20:1 suggests prerenal cause (dehydration, CHF).

low.text: Low urea: Low protein intake, liver disease (reduced production), overhydration, pregnancy.

remarks.text: Urea 18-55 mg/dL is normal. Varies by protein intake and hydration.
Optimal urea <40 mg/dL
Urea 55-100 mg/dL: May indicate prerenal causes (dehydration, high protein intake, bleeding).
Urea >100 mg/dL: Significant renal impairment or severe prerenal state.

description.text: Uric acid is the end product of purine metabolism. Elevated levels can lead to gout and kidney stones.
Hyperuricemia: >7 mg/dL (male), >6 mg/dL (female). Saturation threshold ~6.8 mg/dL.
Gout treatment target: <6 mg/dL (<360 μmol/L). <5 mg/dL if tophi present.
Associated with: Gout, metabolic syndrome, chronic kidney disease, cardiovascular disease.

high.text: High uric acid: Gout, kidney stones, metabolic syndrome, CKD, diuretic use, alcohol, high purine diet.
>9 mg/dL: Severe hyperuricemia. High risk of gout attacks and uric acid kidney stones.

low.text: Low uric acid: Rare. May indicate Fanconi syndrome, Wilson disease, or severe liver disease.

remarks.text: Adult males: 4.0-8.5 mg/dL (Medscape). Higher than females due to hormonal influences.
Adult females: 2.7-7.3 mg/dL. Lower than males; rises after menopause.
<6 mg/dL: Therapeutic target for gout patients per ACR 2020.
<6.8 mg/dL: Below saturation point. Urate crystals unlikely to form.
7-9 mg/dL: Hyperuricemia (mild-moderate). Asymptomatic usually doesn't require treatment.
>9 mg/dL: Severe hyperuricemia. High risk of gout, kidney stones. Consider treatment.
Male: 238-506 μmol/L (4.0-8.5 mg/dL)
Female: 161-434 μmol/L (2.7-7.3 mg/dL)
<360 μmol/L = <6 mg/dL (gout treatment target)

description.text: UACR detects early kidney damage by measuring small amounts of albumin in urine not detected by dipstick.
First morning void sample preferred. Correlates with 24-hour urine albumin excretion.
Annual screening recommended for ALL diabetic patients (ADA guidelines).
Also elevated in: Hypertension, cardiovascular disease, fever, exercise (transient).

high.text: High UACR: Diabetic kidney disease, hypertensive nephropathy, glomerular disease, cardiovascular disease.
30-300 mg/g: Microalbuminuria - reversible with tight glucose/BP control and ACE inhibitors/ARBs.
>300 mg/g: Macroalbuminuria - significant kidney damage, nephrology referral.

low.text: Normal UACR: No evidence of kidney damage. Continue annual screening in diabetics.

remarks.text: <17 mg/g creatinine: Normal for males.
<25 mg/g creatinine: Normal for females.
<30 mg/g: Normal (combined cutoff). KDIGO/ADA guideline.
30-300 mg/g: Microalbuminuria. Early kidney damage. Annual screening recommended.
>300 mg/g: Macroalbuminuria (overt proteinuria). Significant kidney disease.
>3000 mg/g: Severe albuminuria. Nephrotic range, urgent nephrology referral.
<3.4 mg/mmol = <30 mg/g (SI units)

description.text: Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) essential for DNA synthesis, red blood cell formation, and neurological function.
B12 bound to intrinsic factor absorbed in terminal ileum. Stores last 2-5 years.
Deficiency causes macrocytic anemia and irreversible neurological damage. Check MMA/homocysteine if B12 borderline.

high.text: High B12: Usually supplementation. Rarely myeloproliferative disorders, liver disease. Not typically harmful.

low.text: Low B12: Pernicious anemia, malabsorption (gastric surgery), vegan diet, metformin/PPI use. Check MMA/homocysteine.
Symptoms: Fatigue, anemia, neuropathy, memory loss. Can cause irreversible nerve damage.

remarks.text: Normal B12 >300 pg/mL (>221 pmol/L). Note: B12 >200-300 may still be deficient.
Optimal B12 >400 pg/mL. Lower levels may have neurological symptoms despite 'normal' range.
200-300 pg/mL: Grey zone. Check MMA and homocysteine. May be deficient despite 'normal' B12.
<200 pg/mL (<148 pmol/L): B12 deficiency per BCSH. Sensitivity 90-95% for deficiency.
<100 pg/mL: Severe deficiency. Usually symptomatic (anemia, neuropathy).
Normal B12 >221 pmol/L per BCSH 2014
<148 pmol/L: Deficiency cutoff per BCSH 2014

description.text: Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) is essential for amino acid metabolism, neurotransmitter synthesis, and hemoglobin production.
Active form is pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP). Water-soluble, must be consumed regularly.
Unique among vitamins: both deficiency AND excess can cause peripheral neuropathy.

high.text: High B6: Usually excessive supplementation. >100 μg/L may cause sensory neuropathy (numbness, ataxia).
Unlike other B vitamins, B6 toxicity can occur with chronic high-dose supplementation.

low.text: Low B6: Alcoholism, malabsorption, certain medications (isoniazid, hydralazine, penicillamine), chronic kidney disease.
Symptoms: Microcytic anemia, dermatitis, glossitis, depression, confusion, peripheral neuropathy.

remarks.text: ≥30 nmol/L: Adequate status per EFSA 2016 and NIH
20-125 nmol/L: Reference range per Cleveland Clinic
<3.4 μg/L (<20 nmol/L): Deficiency per Labcorp. Associated with anemia and neuropathy.
3.4-5.1 μg/L: Marginal status. May be inadequate for some metabolic functions.
>5.1 μg/L: Adequate status per EFSA. PLP (active form) best marker.
20-50 μg/L (≥30 nmol/L): Optimal per EFSA 2016. Associated with lowest cardiovascular risk.
>50-100 μg/L: Possible excess from supplementation. Monitor for toxicity symptoms.
>100 μg/L: Risk of sensory neuropathy from chronic excess supplementation.

description.text: 25-hydroxyvitamin D [25(OH)D] is the best marker of vitamin D status. Essential for bone health and immune function.
Vitamin D deficiency causes rickets (children) and osteomalacia (adults). Also associated with increased risk of infections, autoimmune disease.
2024 Endocrine Society guideline: Routine testing NOT recommended for healthy adults. Focus on high-risk populations.

high.text: High Vitamin D: Usually from supplementation. >150 ng/mL (375 nmol/L) may cause hypercalcemia, kidney stones.
Vitamin D toxicity is rare but can cause nausea, confusion, arrhythmias, tissue calcification.

low.text: Low Vitamin D: Limited sun exposure, dark skin, malabsorption (celiac, Crohn), obesity, chronic kidney/liver disease.
Deficiency causes bone pain, muscle weakness, increased fracture risk, impaired immunity.

remarks.text: <12 ng/mL (<30 nmol/L): Severe deficiency. Rickets in children, osteomalacia in adults.
12-20 ng/mL (30-50 nmol/L): Deficiency. Increased risk of bone disease. Treat with supplementation.
20-30 ng/mL (50-75 nmol/L): Insufficiency. Suboptimal for bone and overall health per IOM.
30-60 ng/mL (75-150 nmol/L): Sufficiency per Endocrine Society. Adequate for bone health.
40-60 ng/mL (100-150 nmol/L): Optimal functional range. May provide additional non-skeletal benefits.
60-100 ng/mL (150-250 nmol/L): Upper normal range. No known toxicity in this range.
100-150 ng/mL (250-375 nmol/L): Potentially excessive. Monitor for hypercalcemia symptoms.
>150 ng/mL (>375 nmol/L): Toxicity risk. Hypercalcemia, kidney stones, tissue calcification possible.
<50 nmol/L: Deficiency per Endocrine Society
50-75 nmol/L: Insufficiency per IOM
75-150 nmol/L: Sufficiency per Endocrine Society
>375 nmol/L: Potential toxicity

description.text: Zinc is essential for immune function, protein synthesis, wound healing, DNA synthesis, and cell division.
Deficiency causes: Growth retardation, hair loss, diarrhea, immune deficiency, skin lesions, hypogeusia (loss of taste).
High-risk groups: Vegetarians, alcoholics, malabsorption (Crohn's, celiac), elderly, pregnant women.
Sources: Meat, shellfish, legumes, nuts, seeds. RDA: 8-11 mg/day for adults.

high.text: High zinc: Rare. Usually from excessive supplementation. May cause copper deficiency.

low.text: Low zinc: Dietary deficiency, malabsorption, alcoholism, liver disease, sickle cell disease, pregnancy, acrodermatitis enteropathica (genetic).

remarks.text: 80-120 µg/dL (mcg/dL): Normal serum zinc (NIH). Important for immune function and wound healing.
99-130 µg/dL: Optimal range per OptimalDX for best immune function.
60-80 µg/dL: Mild deficiency (subclinical). May affect immune function.
<60 µg/dL: Deficient. Associated with growth retardation, alopecia, diarrhea, immune dysfunction.
<50 µg/dL: Severe deficiency. High risk of complications.
12-18 µmol/L = 80-120 µg/dL (conversion factor: 1 µmol/L = 6.54 µg/dL)
<9 µmol/L = <60 µg/dL (deficient)
